Perhaps I'm a tech idiot, but I'm having great frustration with the Audacity software for Imac. My goal with this turntable was to 1. transfer vinyl tracks to MP3 file 2. move to Itunes library. I'm willing to do the intermediate step of burning to CD if I have to. But after finally successfully recording my first LP to Audacity, I'm at a loss as to how to 1. find it and save as MP3 files 2. identify and label individual tracks 3. move to Itunes or, if I must, burn to cd first so I can then move to Itunes There's apparently something called LameLib that is required. I've consulted the online Audacity tutorial but I guess step by step instructions for these pretty basic goals was too much to ask. It's hard to recommend this turntable, at least with Audacity, for the non-tech savvy consumer.

I took up the ukulele only several months ago as a diversion for upcoming recuperation from back surgery. I started with a "beginner" grade Oscar Schmidt tenor from our local guitar store after trying out several brands. I got pretty good, and decided to upgrade to an acoustic electric and bought this OU7TE from Amazon and a nice used amp from the local store. The store owner had never stocked or played one of these ukes, and even he was impressed with the quality, beauty, and sound that came from this instrument. I look forward to playing it every day, and get compliments from everyone who looks and listens to it. These look and sound much more expensive than they are, and for the money you won't go wrong with one of these beautiful, well-made instruments!

Ruth Etting was born on November 23 1896 and died September 24th 1978 This famous torch singer sang on radio and in Chicago nightclubs before making her Broadway debut in Ziegfeld Follies Of 1927, in which she made a tremendous impact with "Shaking The Blues Away". In her next show, Whoopee 1928 , she introduced "Love Me Or Leave Me", which was subsequently always associated with her. After launching two more future standards, "Get Happy" Nine-Fifteen Revue and "Ten Cents A Dance" Simple Simon , her sparkling rendition of an old Nora Bayes number, "Shine On Harvest Moon", in Ziegfeld Follies Of 1931, made the song a hit all over again. By then she was one of America's brightest stars with her own radio shows and string of hit records. In the 30s she also made three popular movies, Roman Scandals, Hips, Hips, Hooray!, and Gift Of Gab. In 1936 she appeared on the London stage in Transatlantic Rhythm. A year later she split from her husband and manager, Martin "Moe The Gimp" Snyder, a Chicago "hood" who had guided her career from the start. After Ruth Etting's career faded towards the end of the 30s, she entertained at intervals during World War II and enjoyed a brief comeback in the late 40s. This digital release from Vintage Recordings features many of her hits from the 1920's. A great job of removing most of the surface noise and pops and clicks from the original discs. Ruth's voice seems more vibrant and has a great deal of presence in this release. Another excellent release from Vintage Recordings!

I wonder how many of these reviews are written by actual fans of the band--meaning people who understand that this is a band who will always be pressing forward into new directions. Let's be clear this is NOT a heavy metal album. It's not even a rock album. It's ROCK OPERA. If you go into the experience knowing that, and are okay with that, then this album will not disappoint. It is a concept album a term thrown around pretty loosely these days in every sense of the word. Long, poetic, grandiose, multiple characters and using just about every instrument that I personally am familiar with, I find myself lulled into a state of semi-consciousness every time I listen to it. Now, being a true concept album, it's made to be listened to in its totality, or at least in goodish chunks because there is a rhythm, even a balance, to the songs. Another complaint I've read about is the clich storyline. While there is some truth in that, the way I see it is, these guys aren't storytellers, they're musicians, and the story is simply a vehicle for their musical talents, which simply put, are astronomical. I've been trying to figure out which I like more, "The Astonishing" or their previous concept album "Metropolis Pt. 2". They are different beasts with different tones. With "Metropolis", I felt like I will by the fireside listening to a spooky story. With "The Astonishing", I feel like I'm in a theater being blown away by an ensemble performance. Different methods, same effect. If I had one critique for the album, I would say that I might have been helpful to bring on some guest vocalists to handle some of the auxiliary characters as it does get a bit confusing during the POV shifts. Other than that though, "The Astonishing" is EPIC.

Update, I am still really impressed with this printer. However, after some use, the extruder heater element came loose during printing and was glowing red hot!. So, be careful and make sure the heater doesn't come loose. This could be a significant fire hazard. And, as far as I can tell, there is no safety on this product to turn it off if something wrong happens. Be warned. This is a good 3d printer for the price. If you need any minor missing parts, order them. It will be faster. I had some missing xbeam nuts and the belt material was too short for both x and y axis. As soon as I discovered issues I ordered parts. As I expected, I emailed HICTOP with the address in the documentation. They wanted photos etc. I still do not have replacement parts from them. So, expect poor customer service. I had an issue where the x and y pullies wouldn't tighten. The allen wrench didn't fit right. The new belt kit came with pullies and they worked great. I did have to drill out the holes to the parts that captivate the Z axis nuts for the screw drive. I still have a squeek, but it works. So what. Over all, this is a good value for the money. Take your time putting the kit together. Pay attention to the details. Especially the wiring. I used many extra zip ties. The instructions don't tell you how to harness the wires. It will become obvious once the wires are properly connected. I recomend labeling the wires at the connectors so you know what they are once harnessed. I ended up zip tieing the exdruder z-axia cable to the middle of the top bar. Previously, it would snag. The y axis plate would snag too. I used several rubber bands to pull it up so it wouldn't jam. Hopefully the photo will help.

Water water everywhere is right... But that's not all of it... It does come with 6 heads for the water flosser. Easy to switch out. Holds 20oz of water, which isn't much when you 'prime' the unit, but refill after that and 20oz lasts a decent while. I'd say 3-4 flossings. The tooth brush charges in the stand attached to the base. All good things, but that's where it winds down. There are 10 pressure settings for the water pick. The button on the wand allows you to stop water flow while you are holding it down. With that in mind, unless you hold the button down BEFORE AND AS you turn it on...water everywhere. After figuring out the button I wanted to see the thing in action! I turned it down to the lowest setting as directions state . Tested the pressure on my hand, nice a soft. Gentle pulsing... on high it even blasts away old toothpaste left on the sink from six inches away. So leaning into the mirror, I aimed the head every so precisely, released the button on the wand...and....WATER PARK IN THE BATHROOM! My mirror went from just being cleaned from chores an hour before my package arrived to looking like the kids just got done filling water balloons! I know, I know, close your d mn mouth and use the thing blindly, right? Don't get to see it work, but heck no more mess! That SUCKS in my opinion. Not my style. I am trying to figure a way to maybe use it in the shower if I can figure out how not to electrocute myself trying to improve my hygiene. Hope it helps Thanks for reading!

Zephyr 21202 Metal Handle Brace for Push Brooms Hardware House LLC 133234 Replacement Broom Brace Pros - The advantage of this item over similar braces is the low per-unit cost. That is, if you need three or more broom braces, this item a dozen is a good deal. - Securely installed, they definitely strengthen the connection between the handle and the tool head. The handle won't unscrew, and it's very unlikely that it will bend break where it meets the broom head. - The steel is zinc-plated and bendable. Each brace that I used needed to be bent to fit the angle width of the broom head. The best approach is to clamp the bracket in a heavy vise and use a hammer to bend it as needed. Pliers or other tools can also be used. Aluminum broom brackets are easier to bend but are prone to breakage with repeated bending. That's not the case with steel. - Can be used on things other than a broom. E.g., brackets worked fine on a 2' wide squeegee with a long handle. Cons - The supplied wing-nuts are cheap stamped sheet metal. The slotted screws to hold the bracket to the broom head should be a larger diameter. - The brace, while OK, should be thicker wider steel. Other - If you only need one brace, this one has better hardware, the bent section for the handle area is a better design, and it's painted nbsp Hardware House LLC 133234 Replacement Broom Brace Summary Good buy if you need multiple braces.

This is a gift for my son for his good year in school, so its a guitar more for beginners. I just received it, so no in depth user review. 1. Issues - one post in the bridge is not 100 per cent straight it is a little bit angled to the body , but the bridge functions ok and I could raise or lower the action on the bridge without problems. Anyway, a liiiittle bit of a sloppy work. Another issue is a dead note on 14th fret on low E string. I presume the next fret should be filed at the edge, because most probably its too high, but I'll have to bring the guitar to a tech about it and spend some more to fix that. 2. Overall - its a bolt on, 1v,1t pot guitar with a sweetch between them, the switch is positioned ok, the axe is light, with pointy horns and silver bevels on the fretboard that looks stunning, the action was a bit high solved easily and without buzz , the sustain is really good, humbuckers are nothing to write home about, but are more than decent for this price, they actually sound fairly good. The guitar is not the most comfortable to sit with, but it works ok, and because of its looks I wouldn't complain at all about it. The sound without the amp was very nice. With amp it was good, not too bright, not too low. I would change the tuners, maybe the plastic nut and humbuckers if u ask me , but for a beginner the stock set will do just fine, actually very fine. This is a good guitar for the price. I wish I had one like this when I was a kid

nbsp I purchased this retro-reflective tape to put on my nbsp Rubbermaid 1172 ActionPacker Storage Box, 24 Gallon nbsp that fits within my nbsp Aosom Wanderer Bicycle Bike Cargo Luggage Trailer - Black nbsp just to help keep me safer on the road by being more noticeable especially from the side. I do have a 3 piece orange flag on my bike trailer and in addition some reflectors,a retro-reflective rear slow moving vehicle sign and 2 of these LED flasher lights on back of it. Bike Runner's Safety Light 4 Pack - Flashing 5 Red LED, 7 Function, 6 Patterns Red Cover for Outdoor Runner's, Jogger's Walker's with Arm Strap, Belt Clip and Bike Mount nbsp in addition to rear flashing lights on my bicycle. I know this may seem like overkill however again I wanted something noticeable on the sides of the bike trailer container. There may be a rare incident where I do have to ride with this trailer at night hopefully not though. Even so I like being prepared for that eventuality. i know. who cares about my personal listening history with sufjan stevens...? somehow, it seems relevant to me. i bought "seven swans" upon its release all those years ago. i played it i liked it. i liked some of it a lot. i was not driven to purchase his other releases. what did i care about michigan and illinois e ? then i saw that some magazine had rated "the age of adz" as the album of the year. i was curious. i bought it. i thought, "this guy is either a genius or the biggest show-off of all time." after a few more listens, i was flabbergasted and was forcing people to listen. i had to know if others heard what i heard....if they would be as overwhelmed as i was... needless to say, it didn't take me long to go out and get "illinoise", "michigan", "enjoy your rabbit", "the avalanche", "a sun came", and, finally, "all delighted people." i have the christmas set on order. the "bqe" will be next. somehow i know it. i see the number of reviews some of sufjan stevens' albums have received, and i was shocked to see how few reviews have been written about "all delighted people." i know i've played it fifty times in the past few weeks. i wonder if people are avoiding it because of it being labelled an ep. it's over an hour of incredible, intense, beautiful, heart-crushing, unbelievable music. i think, with this one, i've answered my own question. he may be a show-off....he MAY be, but, without a doubt, something is going on here. this man is shocking in his ambition...and he pulls it all off. i am astonished i find myself listening in disbelief. occasionally i have flashes of certain phases of todd rundgren at other times, i am reminded of van dyke parks' "song cycle," but, ultimately, this is music that, as far as i know, could only have been created by sufjan stevens. i haven't been this musically obsessed in a long time, and it feels really good. - throughout this intense avalanche of his music, "all delighted people" seems to be my favorite. i wonder how different the listening experience would have been if i had listened to each one upon its release instead of bombarding myself with all of it pretty much all at once. for one thing, i might be less exhausted from the intensity. i vote "genius."

Overall this is an excellent portable, introductory 61-key keyboard. Some things I like 1 All the buttons are easy to use. That was my 1 criteria. Most keyboards, I think, are hard to figure out, but this one is pretty intuitive. It's easy to change instruments, songs, etc. You will not want to lose the instruction book though. There's just a few things that I use rarely that I need to pull out the instruction book to remember. 2 Split keyboard -- you can play one instrument, like bass or cello, on the left hand, and another instrument like piano, on the right hand, with the touch of just a few buttons. 3 built-in metronome for learning. There's also a built-in lesson plan, but haven't really used it. 4 Many of the instruments are really good, especially the horns and organ sounds. 5 Auto chord-fill. Don't know how to fill in a chord? No problem. Just hit any one or two keys on the left hand, and the keyboard fills in the rest of the chord for you. Some things I don't like 1 the "realistic" grand piano sound is not particularly realistic. 2 the volume is very low, even plugged in. I have to turn the sound up to about 75 of maximum to get the sound of an acoustic piano playing mezzoforte mf . That surprised me given how large the speakers are. 3 the piano does not come with any of the essential addons. Not even a power cord!! No MIDI cord. No sustain pedal. All of these essential addons cost bucks. The power cord is 16. Add MIDI and Sustain 40 20 , and you now add almost 50 to the price of this keyboard. Oh, and don't forget the stand, that's not included either! Re the comments, I should be clear that I didn't get this from Amazon, I got it from a store, and that was the one that did not include a power cord or any other attachments. I don't know if Amazon is including a power cord or not, but the regular store box does not.

After using this tape for my recent head unit install, I can't go back to using electric tape again. Although electric tape can be useful in many automotive applications, I have found it certain applications -- such as wrapping wires under the engine bay -- it gets very gooey easily and is a pain in the you know what to undo. This Tesa tape is very heat resistant 150 C or 302 F and wrapped around itself very easily w no gooey mess left behind. This tape has been very useful when wrapping the amplifier power wire under the engine bay by that I ran from the battery terminal post to the amplifier in the vehicle. Moreover, it give the installation a professional look. I have posted some pictures of my usage of this product and hope it helps give an idea of the possibilities of this tape. Note There are different models of Tesa tape so make sure to to get the one that fits your usage. For example, if you wanted to predominantly use this under your engine bay, then the "Tesa 51026 or 51036" would be great as they are heat resistant up too 302 F. But if you mainly want to use this in cooler temperatures -- such as around the home or the passenger area of a vehicle -- then the "Tesa 51608 or 51606" which are cheaper would more than suffice as they are heat resistant up too 221 F.

My five-star rating is based on what my ears hear when my guitars are plugged into an Axe FX-II with the main XLR outputs connected to a pair of HS8 studio monitors. No matter what patch I throw at them the HS8 does the Axe justice. I vacillated for several months while researching studio monitors in the 300 to 700 price range and took a leap of faith on Yamaha's reputation on previous models. After a month of daily use, my ears tell me I made the right choice. I like to record my practice sessions so that I can critically listen to my performance, zeroing in on what sounds good and where I need improvement. When creating new patches with different pedals, amps, and cabinets, I'm confident that the HS8 monitors are faithfully reproducing the intended tone and sonic delivery. There's no question in my mind that I wouldn't hesitate in buying another pair if and when I require them. For the money the HS8 monitors are an excellent choice.

When I first saw the Broadway musical I was blown away by the energy of the performances so much that I actually overlooked the music a bit. But now after listening to the soundtrack, I must say it is phenomenal and one of the best I've ever had the fortune to listen to. Like traditional Broadway shows there are certain musical themes that are repeated and rebuilt upon itself. But like hiphop, the music is raw and exciting, entertaining, smart and clever. There are so much amazing music that makes you laugh, cheer, weep, or just groove to the beats. If nothing else, it gives you an appreciation of how great the founding fathers were during the American Revolution. USA! USA! It really makes you want to crack open a history book and read up about the early stories of our country. But this is really all thanks to the amazing genius of the creator and star of Hamilton, Lin-Maneul Miranda. In less talented hands the music and story could have come off as silly, campy or just simply ill suited. But he masterfully has something for everyone. Heart breaking ballads? Check. Hilarious monologues? Check. Rousing choruses? Check. Epic Rap battles? Double Check! I have literally not been able to stop listening to this music.

You might say that Tommy Roe was ahead of the curve. He recorded "bubblegum" music before the term was even coined. After his wildly successful debut single, 1962's "Sheila", Tommy Roe went on to make 21 more appearances on the Billboard Hot 100 Singles chart over the next decade. A strong argument can be made that his 1966 Top Ten smash "Sweet Pea" was really the very first "bubblegum" hit. Recall that the term "bubblegum" did not appear until the heyday of Buddah records in 1968-1969 with groups like Ohio Express and the 1910 Fruitgum Co. Over the next few years Tommy Roe would go on to make several more of those "bubblegum" records that were so popular among young teens back in the day. Perhaps my favorite of these is "Hooray For Hazel". Released as the follow-up to "Sweet Pea" in September of 1966 "Hooray For Hazel" would become another Top Ten smash spending a total of 13 weeks on The Billboard Hot 100 Singles chart peaking at 6. This is the original hit single recording you would remember from the radio. Lots of fun!

I recently added a pH meter with an analog output to this meter that I placed in the garden on a pvc pipe mount so that could see it from the kitchen. I have it running on a separate 24V DC supply since there was an oddity with the backlight affecting my analog signal from the meter, causing it to blip up and down a couple of units randomly. I believe it was a flaky on-board power supply from the pH meter. In any event, isolating it and hooking up the backlight has given me 24 7 access to the pool pH and it is very clear and bright day or night. The meter has very accurate pots to align the output with the analog output and it was very simple to wire and configure. Compared to typical industrial analog indicators with similar functionality but five times the price, this is the way to go.

Vassily Primakov born 1979 in Moscow, Russia is an award-winning Russian concert pianist and recording artist known for his interpretations of Chopin. Primakov began studies at Moscow's Central Special Music School at age eleven as a pupil of Vera Gornostayeva. He then attended the Music Academy of the West in Santa Barbara and the Juilliard School in New York, where he studied under Jerome Lowenthal. In 1999 Primakov won second prize at the Cleveland International Piano Competition, and in 2001 he was a semi-finalist in the Van Cliburn Competition. Upon hearing Primakov's performance of "La Valse" at the competition, Van Cliburn himself said "Prodigious technique, really wonderful," with a "look of sheer rapture on his face." Primakov then won both the Silver Medal and Audience Prize at the 2002 Gina Bachauer International Piano Competition. In 2007 he was honored by the Classical Recording Foundation with their Young Artist of the Year Award. His disc of 21 Mazurkas by Chopin was named a "Best of Year" by National Public Radio 2009 . In 2011, along with his duo partner Natalia Lavrova, Mr. Primakov launched a new Record Label LP Classics.

OK, These are marketed by several distributors and are re-branded with individual badges. Rather than write a paragraph that gives general impressions I am choosing to give a feature by feature rating which I feel reflects the product more. Build quality Fair to good. They are of an all plastic design but being VERY small that is OK. The mount while adequate is very flimsy. Brightness Small venue only. The 10 watt rating is not accurate. If all the chips are on RGBW 255 max it draws about 5-6 watts. If only one chip is used they vary from 3 to 4 watts draw. It is a very focused beam 4-5 degrees so they are useful in a small venue. Light Quality Each chip is individually controllable so colors are good and flexible and can be blended. However, there is a slight de-centering from each chip when blended that inhibits a hard focus and causes a color fringe at the edges of the light pool. Hazing allows the visual effect of "seeing" the light beam and adds much to the effect of a pinspot. DMX control Its ok. With 6 channels I feel it is a bit on the sloppy side. Channel one has three functions that aren't well combined as strobe, brightness overall, and full on are all combined on this slider. Two through five are pretty straight forward as RGBW one channel each. Channel 6 is the most confusing of all with as near as I can tell has about 8 functions. Various combinations of fading in and out of different colors randomly and speed of flashing and even sound control so there is a lot of playing around with this to get desired results. Auto run Has two main chase functions as well as single color modes. I don't use this so I guess it is OK. Conclusion They are OK and can be useful on small stages. Great for static beam effects or random strobing when multiples are used. As hair lights for static musicians that don't move around are superb. Mine is a static stage in a church so they aren't moved around much so build quality isn't as much a factor. Longest throw for me is about 18' and we can darken the building well so with hazing the stage we can create some useful effects. Best feature CHEAP

I have the strong impression that many of those bad reviews for this printer are made by people who are totally new to 3d printing. One must have to point out that there is a serious learning curve to 3d printing, and that there are many, many variables one has to keep in mind, from the way things are constructed, over material properties, to print bed adhesion, bridging capabilities, object to print bed head orientation, and print setting such as support structures, infill, rafts, etc. to name just a few. So if you are a newbie, it's easy to blame the machine. The fact however is that the Robox is one of the most easy-to-use, plug-and-play 3D printers on the market, and definitely worth the money. I have been a Kickstarter backer for the very first Robot back in 2014 2015, and yes, they had a bumpy start. When I received my first Robox, I was frustrated as well. The problem back then however wasn't the hardware, but the software. Plus my lack of understanding of 3d printing in general. The software AutoMaker became better and better, and is constantly being improved. And so did my knowledge about how to design for 3d printing and how to get satisfying results. By 2017 I own no less than six Roboxes, given to the fact that they cost way less than an Ultimaker, however provide comparable results.

I have now on my 2nd roll of this filament. I use it with my Makerbot Replicator 2 3D printer. Im not a 3d printing guru or anything and when I 1st started printing with this filament I had alot of problems with it adhering to the build plate and curling etc. After alot of fustration etc I discovered it was because I was using a too high extruder temperature.. Since I dialed the extruder temp down to 215 220C 97 of my problems seem to have disappeared. The finish on this is a gloss black. Note also I had to print a custom spool holder to be able to mount the Zen Toolworks spool on the back of my Rep2.. There are plenty options for these on Thingiverse ...

I installed Cubase 8.01 as directed on my Dell Optiplex 990 with a 64 bit Windows 7 system. It took a while. The registration menu came up and I filled in the registration code. I then clicked the icon on my desktop and a dialog box with this message came up "This Steinberg product does not support Basic High Contrast Themes on Windows operating Systems. Please use one of the Areo themes that you can set in the Windows Control Panel." When I clicked the "OK" the program simply quit. Did this a few times. I tried uninstalling and reinstalling it and the result was the same. The Steinberg website was not helpful, because for some unrelated reason it wouldn't let me activate an account, which is what you must do in order to get customer support. The website has an automated account registration process where, after giving them your email and a password, it is supposed to send you a confirmation email with an activation link. The website says it sent me the email, but it didn't no, it's not in my spam inbox . The account page has a "resend" button, in case you didn't get the confirmation email. But, alas, the result is the same. The account page has a third option, in case nothing else works it gives you an email address. I tried it, but it's been a whole day and nobody has replied. Of course, the website is in Germany and Germans don't work on Saturdays. I will give them until Monday, but at this point it looks like it's going back to Amazon Earlier versions of Cubase have been great, so here's hoping Steinberg customer support, and the product itself, aren't as bad as they seem. Stay tuned...

Not for the heavy handed, but IMHO they work great for pick or strum. I use these picks the most, and give them 5 stars, in spite the easy breakage factor. Don't really know a way around it, since the hole is naturally going to make it weaker across that area. I just don't grip the pick that high, and so not much stress across hole line. Mine seem to break from the outside inward, but seem to get a fair amount of play out of them before they fail. And like another reviewer mentioned, they are responsive enough that they seem to punch the wound strings a bit. So that's the semi - CON side. I don't wail on my picks, so the PROS side easily outways any cons. They really do improve my grip and orientation. and l Iike finger pressure feedback connection in the middle star hole. Discovered an extra bonus I use the strap locks on my Tele. to hang a spare star pick. It's easy to modify the star hole to perfectly snap onto my strap lock knob. I always have a spare, real close at hand, dangling on the strap lock knob, easy to snag,. You just simply rotate a small kitchen sharpening "steel" tapered round fine ultra-fine cut file, used to sharpen good kitchen knives , and turn it slowly and gently, smooth cut, precisely honing the tips of the star off -- just enough to allow the pick to easily slide on and off the strap lock knob, without falling off too easily. Have to go slow and take the time to adjust the hole size perfectly, loose fit It has to easily roll off the pull knob smoothly, without any possibility of yanking out the release pin on the strap lock. Best to use little to no pressure on the steel while starting rotating, other than it's own weight. Regular wood rat tail file way too agressive, 500 sandpaper not easy symmetrical results. Takes some light an patience, but easy to do a small batch in 5-10 min. using the steel . No matter where got pick, backups nearby.

I have only had this vacuum for a few hours so my review will not include long term results. The photos attached show what was picked up by this vacuum after I had swept with our 3 year old non-pet Bissell after having replaced the belt this past week and still having problems with it. We have 2 large dogs, a black haired German Shepherd that sheds ridiculously, a brown haired Chocolate Lab who sheds moderately and a gray cat that does not shed noticably enough to note. The dogs track a lot of mud through the house and into the carpet that turns to dust once dry. We have new brown short shag carpet. I am beyond happy to have picked up all that's missed every time I vacuum with the Bissell. The machine is powerful and heavy to push. The retractable cord is convenient and doesn't tangle easily, which is nice but I hate that I have to pull the cord all the way out every time- just something new to get used to. I love the snap-in feature for the handheld hose- it locks in when you put it back so you know you're getting the most suction if it's locked in. To dump the dirt out, all you have to do is hold it over the trash can and push a button and the base drops out. I was not ready for this, as you can tell by my now dirty trashcan! It is really convenient though, and now I know! All I used so far was the hose and extension in corners and the suction is awesome and gets crevices well. Happy with the price as well- I got it on lightening deal for about 85. Edit I have photos for this review, but cannot figure out where the photo upload is.

What first appealed to me when I listened through Travel I EP was the classical vibe coming through each track. Future of Forestry was going for a very grand and epic sound with rock----epic like a movie soundtrack. Continuing to experiment with Christmas music, the moment has finally come for 8 tracks from previous releases to be reconstructed as classical pieces with nothing but strings, a piano and Eric's voice. And it's perfect. Each track is a nice surprise. I wasn't sure how "All I Want" was going to be pulled off with such a simple musical arrangement, but its original tone, I think, is even more so enhanced by the classical setup. "Hallelujah" keeps the epic tone and feel, and every other track sounds, truly, beautiful. This is a wonderful side to Future of Forestry. They are one of the most talented acts out there.

I was pleasantly surprised by this album. All the symphonies are premiere recordings which deserve wider exposure. Herschel is less influnced by Handel or Haydn than by the Mannheim School, as befits a German born composer. Melodically, the works are charming rather than memorable. It is the rhythmic innovations and interesting harmonies which provide these symphonies with real zest. Herschel clearly had a keen mind, at work musically before his career as an astronomer. I was very pleased with these performances, although I think the innovative quality of this music could be brought out even further on period instruments. For the time being, though, this will certainly do, and the sound engineering is very fine, also.

I've been playing the guitar about 45 years, acoustic, electric, and nylon string. I like Korg tuners and find them the best, particularly for the money, not only for my guitars but also for my fiddle and my banjos. They are small, sturdy, and no frills. They are accurate and inexpensive. They work easily. This is the only real guitar tuner that I like. I have other guitar tuners that are pretty good that are not really guitar tuners but chromatic tuners with guitar settings. This is small accurate and sturdy and fits easily inside your shirt pocket or inside the small compartments in a guitar case. It does have features for other settings which are great for alternate tunings. Don't be fooled by the Intellitouch and other strap ons. You can't figure out all their arrows as well as the dial on Korgs and others. They are too much trouble to use especially is you are doing precision work like setting up an adjustable bridge. The only thing is that they can be a bit unreliable if the a battery gets worn down. I usually check in new batteries every time I am going out to play some place serious. With the discount prices you can find in shops and around the web, the only way to beat the price on these tuners is to steal one!

Vancouver based Terrence O'Brien is the sole creator of Anomalous Disturbances and "Archive Two" is marked as his 5th solo release from 2009, covering selected recordings from the period 2001-2008, some of these tracks are previously unreleased, some appear in edited versions and some are taken from various compilation appearances. I must say it's a very nice idea to put all these tracks on one double disc set, a dream of each collector!!! Even if centered around deeper atmosonics, the music of Anomalous Disturbances is quite diverse when crossing through several styles and moods. Disc One floates from deeply organic oriented washes on "Portent v. 2 Omen Mix " to massive and hypnotic celestials on "The Secret Adam", from flatlined futuristic dronescapes on "Quickenberries" to warmer nostalgic and cinematic "Noodles" and from minimal mysterious experiments on "Everybody's Talkin'v. 2" to absolutely tranquil and blissful drifts on 18-minute long "Daydream 3". That's it for the first part of this collection and I can say that I am absolutely impressed and deeply immersed. Now my expectations are even higher, so let's investigate the second chapter. Disc Two unfolds with ethereal and expansive sound waves of "Sedna", a true deep space voyage! Highly evocative "Low Pulse" keeps on circulating and evolving, just like the next one, darker "Mysterium Tremendum 23", with its strong submerging effect, quite cinematic too. "Live At Soma Cafe Pt. 2" and "Segue To Heaven" are two beautifully absorbing intergalactic travels while the deeper droning "Space Elevator Muzik" closes nicely this fantastic journey. Yes, I am done with the second CDr and I must say that my best expectations were surpassed. Even if all these pieces have been recorded over differing time span, they all work compactly together. I think Terrence O'Brien's guitar originated virtuoso performance remains still quite unrecognized, so it's time to explore the soundworlds of Anomalous Disturbances, it's highly rewarding!!! Trust me, this is another Canadian ambient must have!!! And while on this exciting journey, don't miss his newest collaborative effort with Matthew Poulakakis entitled Wassonic, released in March 2011. Richard Grtler Bratislava, Slovakia

I bought this because I needed a nice tape emulation delay pedal. At the same time I was playing around with loop pedals and came across a demo of this on youtube. The pedal is wayyy better than I expected with all features tape delay, looper, and digital delay - the tape delay heads have individual outputs, so if you have a nice recording gig at home, you can hook up all 5 outputs 1 mix, 1 tape head, 2 tape head, 3 tape head, and 4 tape head to a 8 input mixer interface and create swirling, panning, delay effects. Its really cool if you have it all hooked up and setup correctly. Im currently using this as my outboard delay effect that I route through the aux channels I use a MOTU 828 firewire . The looper is great as long as you have rhythm and know when to press record and play to start and end phrases. Again, I was very happy that this pedal exceeded my expectations. NOTE TO DELAY TWEEKERS meaning you like to turn the knobs while the delay is on to create crazy sounds... This is not the pedal for you. When you turn the delay settings while a signal is going through it, the delay effect is reset every time you make an adjustment. So you can not make those crazy delay tweeking swoops and robotic metal chainsaw oscillations like some other delay pedals...

Especially when it comes to jazz and funk,I'm always excited to here about an album I never knew existed. And for that I never was even aware of this album until several years ago,not too long before Wounded Bird reissued it on CD. This came out the year of my birth. And over the years I've come to understand this wasn't the most comfortable era if you were a band playing funk music. The result of the whole "disco sucks" fiasco basically kept any uptempo R B styles off of radio. I've heard it called by some "the freeze out". Interestingly enough this was when most bands tended to be at a certain creative height. For Royce Royce it was somewhat sad because they lost their original lead singer Gwen Dickey prior to this release. In the end they chose Richee Benson as a successor,because they knew they could never replace the apparently fame seeking Dickey. Benson is possessed of a more husky,typical female funk vocalist type of singing style. Luckily she also has a somewhat more jazzy approach to her singing. And that worked out fine because the band themselves were facing some of the biggest changes of their ever changing career. As with their previous album and their second this album opens with a ballad "And You Wish For Yesterday". Honestly I've always had the unfortunate habit of skipping over the slow numbers on funk albums. Which is sometimes a mistake because,for example what would EWF's nbsp That's the Way of the World nbsp be without the stratospheric "Reasons"? However songs such as the title song "Love Is In The Air" and "Would You Please Be Mind" are the main reason for the four stars. None of these ballads has much character lyrically or musically beyond what the band had done. And NONE hold a CANDLE frankly to "I'm Going Down","I'm Wishing On A Star" and "Love Don't Live Here Anymore". All the same the uptempo numbers capture the transition much better. A good example is the boogie style of "I Wanna Make It You". The more synthesized style of boogie funk suits Rose Royce pretty well actually. "Funkin' Around",a witty story about smoking a joint in an airplane reflects producer Norman Whitfield's ever present love of the P-Funk sound,even as George Clinton's musical empire was crumbling at this point. "You're A Winner" of course not only sounds the most like the bands earlier funk but blends some of that boogie style in with the classic rhythmic crunch a well. The album closes with the surprisingly retro for 1980 funky soul of "Help Yourself",sounding somewhat more like 1970 than 1980. Now there's no question this isn't going to go down in history as one of Rose Royce's classic albums. And trust me they have more than a handful of those. But this is actually a lot more powerful than one might expect it to be. They were facing difficult musical times. And adding to that the extra responsibility of breaking in a new lead singer with Richee Benson. It is kind of sad that they only seemed to be able to strengthen their funk numbers and not deliver a complete album package,resulting in a handful of rather weak slow jams. But they'd done that well in the past. And the energy and drive that characterized the band from the get go wasn't gone yet. So you could kind of call it their first transitional album. That considering they'd wind up recording on up to the end of 80's. And that was likely a good cut off point as well. It's one of their least known albums. And while it's not exactly a full on lost treasure the high quality material here IS of THE highest quality.

I went through 2 other capacitors.. a Boss and a Tsunami.. before settling on this model. Immediately when I opened that Amazon box and saw this nice, wooden box with a latch just for the packaging, I admit that my eyebrows raised. Inside, the mounting hardware is all metal with a securely latching clamp system vs the Boss cheap plastic clamps. Awesome. Included as well were allen keys for all fittings and gasp quality 4AWG cable terminals! That's a first. One thing that struck me is that there's no remote turn on wire.. you simply hook up positive and ground. I wondered how the heck it was going to sense when there was current or if it would stay on forever?? Weird. Hooked it up to charge, and unlike the other caps the voltage meter did NOT come on.. and the lights on the front were blinking while it emitted this weird beeping. I trusted it and kept charging it for around 3 mins as per the instructions. Skeptically, I hooked everything up when done.. and BAM! Correct voltage was displayed and everything was good. Started the vehicle, and everything on the cap went out.. only to come on when needed. Bass is clearer, lights don't dim, ignition doesn't falter.. perfect. End result Awesome quality cap that met and exceeded my expectations. HIGHLY recommended.

The Mother Hips were a favorite of mine for years. Since seeing them on the Horde tour in 1995, I've loved these guys work. Their output from 'Back to the Grotto' through 'Green Hills of Earth' is flawless. Pacific Dust and it's predecessor Kiss the Crystal Flake suffer from uneven songwriting. After spending long periods of time with both albums, I am always 50 percent moved by these albums, and 50 percent disappointed. Track by track 1. White Falcon Fuzz Brilliant, one of Tim's best. The lyrics roll in like waves and swell with emotion. 10 10 2. Third Floor Story Rockin! It's great to have this on a 12" and this version is just as good as the old one. 10 10 3. Jess OXOX A little light rock song. It's nice. 7.5 10 4. The Lion and the Bull meandering and pointless. Greg Loiacono's work is usually very strong... his material on this album lacks focus. 4 10 5. One Way Out another nice light rocker 7.5 10 6. All in Favor Greg's best tune on the album, a nice light rock song 7.5 7. Pacific Dust Weirdo space rocker. Finally a little surge in energy! Give us more of this. 9 10 8. Young Charles Ives horrible. Boring. Meandering. Greg, you have much better material than this. 1 10 9. Are You Free A well done 'in the style of' type of song. Very 70's cheesy AM feeling. It's just fine... but certainly not great. 6 10 10. Cheer up Champ The album closes with this fun tune that has lots of cool breaks, twist and turns. 9 10 Average 71.5 percent. This could be a great record. We know these guys can write memorable music. We know how good of a band the Mother Hips are. It seems like they need a good editor... someone to tell them that a song isn't good enough. If you combine the best tracks on Pacific Dust with the best tracks on Kiss the Crystal Flake, you would have a great record. As it stands, we get another watered down affair. I do like this album better than it's predecessor, but it's mediocre moments drag the high moments down.

At 57 years of age I have more music to listen to than I know what to do with. Back in the early 80's while driving through Boston I heard a song on 'BCN by a new band called Pixies. At the very next record store I would purchase their initial EP and never look back. The Pixies are without a doubt the single most influential alternative band ever. In meeting at that time, Black Francis, at a small club here in Portland, Maine, I would compliment them on their sound and say that they would go on to become one of the more profound bands ever. When they went their separate ways it left a huge hole in the music scene that would never be filled. Their quirky and infectious music would become the banner carried by so many devoted fans. In reuniting they would then have a new audience as well as we have never left their side. This new music is fantastic and it is great to read these reviews and see so many 5 star tributes. The Pixies are indeed this generation's Beatles and please don't take that the wrong way. Thank you so much for making this wonderful music!

I've never used an electric toothbrush before, so alas, I cannot compare this to other brands. However, I can let you in on what a first time user may feel. First off, let's go over the basics. This is a rechargable toothbruth that uses sonic technology to brush up to 31,000 brush strokes a minute. It has a built in timer that will alert you every 30 seconds for 2 minutes. This allows you to time how long and evenly you brush. It's a very helpful feature! It comes with just one removable toothbrush head, so you'll need to buy a new one when bristles start to wear out. For those who have never used a toothbruth, the sensation of this brush might be weird at first. The company thought about new users, the bristles will gradually go faster after a few complete brush cycles. The handle isn't too large to hold comfortablly and I believe the tooth brush head's length makes it easier to reach hard to reach places. One small note, my brush included paperwork on extending the factory warranty. However, it seemed to have expired last year, so sadly, it was no longer redeemable. Overall, I believe this is working quite well. I've noticed less plaque build up, which is fantastic!

I was a bit gun shy about purchasing a Chinese import guitar, having tried in the past with one and got burned took it to my local luthier and he found the bridge plate was missing . But wanting another guitar to keep in an alternate tuning, and not having a big budget, I gave this a try based on some good online reviews. All I can say is everyone who wants a backup or second or third guitar should get one of these. I play fingerstyle ragtime, celtic, blues and slack key, and love the sound and playability of this little instrument. The 000 size and wider neck are perfect for fingerstyle, and my luthier gave it the thumbs up when I took it in for inspection he even declined to set it up, saying it was good to go . My primary guitar is a Taylor GC-7, which I love. Does the RK sound as good? No, but it still sounds and plays really fine. Does the Taylor sound 10 x as good which is just about exactly how much more it cost ? Not by a mile. Do yourself a favor and pick one of these up, and you'll find yourself going to it quite a bit.

Five stars not because this is RC's best album of all time, which it is not, although if you like Cooder, as I do, you will probably dig it. But five stars to try to balance out the indefensible one- and two-star reviews, which are not really reviews at all but attacks on Cooder's political opinions. Of course if you are hostile to RC's politics, you will hate this record. That doesn't mean it is an unsuccessful piece of work it means that you don't agree with its message. Fair and fine. But the critiques offered are not true critiques, merely warmed-over talking points lifted from Rush Limbaugh's newsletter and Fox News. These reviewers blow the whistle on themselves by their broad-brush use of the word "liberal" as an insult. If you have a serious argument to make, you don't start off that way. And in any case, if it weren't for "liberals," the country would have no rural electrification, your aged parents would not have an income from social security, nor would they have help with medical expenses through Medicare, and most of our fathers would not have had a chance at a college education via the G.I. Bill. That's just for starters. So -- please -- enough of this b.s. Stick to whether the record is successful on its own terms -- not whether you agree or not, or how you feel about "liberals," but whether the stuff on the record is any good either as entertainment or propaganda. I can well imagine a Ry Cooder fan thinking this stuff is too heavy-handed, or obvious, or... whatever. Fair enough. I don't feel that way, but such a review could be thought-provoking. But these "anti-liberal" reviews don't have much to do with provoking thought -- just provoking a fight.

I got this as a late birthday gift from a friend. I have only dabbled in playing guitar and that was years ago, I learned a few cords because a roommate had one and showed me. I bought a 12 string at a thrift store pawn shop in Mississippi thinking I would learn and I tightened the strings so tight trying to tune it that the neck broke one day while I was away. That's how clueless I was. But I have hardly been able to put it down since I got it. A friend at work used the tuner to tune it on the first day and attached a little post it note so I can remember which notes are which. It's a perfect beginner instrument. Small enough to carry with you anywhere like a toy. But sounds good enough to make you want to play it like a serious instrument. Just watching the mid level Hawaiian guy at work play it taught me enough to experiment with a few cords. It's so fun to figure out and sounds so good you just don't want to put it down. When it's new the strings stretch a lot and it falls out of tune. But that's normal for a new ukulele according to my Hawaiian friends. But it's so simple and fun to use the built in tuner to get it back in tune that you just don't have to "fret" about it... hhaha..get it? Ok, nevermind. One way or another this is a perfect beginner instrument. And it sounds good enough for the advanced player in my novice opinion. The build quality is really good, finish is very nice and clean subtle. The tiki man etched on the base string bridge looks so cool. All in all a classy fun musical instrument. 2 of the screws on the back of the tuning knobs were a little loose so just wiggle the knobs gently and see if there's movement on the plate. Nothing a careful turn with a small phillips head screwdriver can't fix. If not for that I'd give it 5 stars as a solid entry level ukelele. But what do I know. I'm only a beginner.

If you have a newer TV without any analog outputs and a receiver stereo without optical inputs then you are in luck. This is the best bang for the buck digital to analog converter you can get. FiiO has done an excellent job with this unit, this little box packs an excellent sound quality to my old Sony stereo can even tell the difference in quality comparing to my other home theater with optical inputs. Since my newer LG lcd tv did not have any analog outputs this was required. There is absolutely no noise or hiss when turning up the volume. Only little cons I can think of and are not really cons - It needs to be plugged to an outlet for power so considered that in you setup - Included optical toslink cable is a little thin and can get unplugged easily if you move things around - Bright led when powered could be disctracting for those who cannot have it in the back of your tv or furniture Remember if your tv does not output PCM then you will get no sound as this does not decode DD DTS, so check your TV's manual first. You can also pair this with an old dvd player and your favorite headphones and you got yourself a nice cheap setup for listening to some music. Overall nice little DAC converter, FiiO products are great.

Mine fit on my Epiphone SG Special pretty well---a bit of a squeaker, using the supplied posts in the existing inserts, but fine. I didn't use the inserts that came with the bridge tailpiece, but did replace the posts. The bridge posts seem to be very slightly smaller in diameter and that matters when it's a squeak-fit because the posts are just a fraction of a millimeter further apart than optimal. It did come with the tailpiece as expected somebody commented that theirs didn't, but maybe they didn't order the same exact item? . Unfortunately, out of the box, the bridge exhibited the dreaded ABR-1 bridge buzz in a major way---the screws don't fit snugly in the screw holes and they and or the saddles rattle around a bit. At least some of them did experiments with foam tape to dampen them indicateed that at least one thing is rattling in the upper three and at least one thing is rattling in the lower three. This was very noticeable when playing the SG unplugged. Without the buzz, it's fine for practicing in a quiet room with no amp with it, it's too annoying. There are two standard fixes for this that I learned from Gibson-oriented forums. The first, which I did, is to put kinks in the retaining wire that runs along the top of the screw-head side of the bridge. Do this by using the corner of a small flat screwdriver blade, positioning it where it just catches the wire with the rest of the blade away from the bridge. Whack the screwdriver soundly but not really hard with your hand. BUT PUT SOMETHING RIGID UNDER THERE FIRST to protect your guitar from the screwdriver blade. See the photo, where I use a wooden ruler to protect the guitar... you want to kink the wire down midway between each adjacent pair of screws, making it into a kind of zigzag spring rather than a loose rattly wire. For me, it dramatically reduced the rattling and buzzing that had been driving me nuts. Believe it or not, this is the standard hack for Gibson ABR-1 bridges, not just cheap knockoffs, and people routinely do it to 3,000 Les Pauls. Gibson really needs a better bridge design after all these decades. The other fix for ABR-1 bridge buzz is to use a little bit of clear nail polish to basically glue the saddles and the retaining wire to the bridge, once you've got the intonation right. I didn't do that, but may resort to it if some of the buzzing returns. Later a little buzz did return dependent on whether a screw is in compression turning it a couple of turns loosen and back to tighten made it bind again, but it's annoying . I ordered a Wilkinson roller bridge to replace it but am not returning this ABR-1 because I need the tailpiece and that alone is worth the price. If I were really short of cash I'd have used the nail polish trick.

This review is for the mp3 download of James Brown's 1966 Handful Of Soul Album. A great treat for die hard fans, though only features James playing the organ with his band and guest female vocalists. Not for the average fan expecting the usual JB album. I don't believe this album was ever issued on CD, as most of his other original Smash instrumental album releases, until recently issued on digital dowmload. There are a couple of songs obviously trasferred from vinyl as you can hear the scratches, but that is what makes it nostalgic! My guess is some of the original album master was lost those songs had to be transferred from an LP, but I don't mind. The rest of the album is fine digital quality. Same with his Yesterday, Today Tomorrow album. I am looking forward hopefully to the eventual digital release of JB's final instrumental Smash Album James Brown Plays The Real Thing. For the JB enthusiasts, sit back enjoy!

I bought this cartridge to use with my recently acquired Beatles in Mono LP set. I have other mono on vinyl, but never bothered before. The added advantage is that this is a moving coil, not moving magnet cartridge, which is normally superior for sound resolution and performance. I installed it on a Thorens TD 147 Mk. II 1985 on a spare TP63 tonearm wand I had purchased some time ago. The results are outstanding, a clear and vibrant soundstage, I thought it was a bit light on the bass but then I found this was not the case as I moved through the mono collection. I am using a NAD phono stage, make sure that what you are using has inputs for a MC cartridge. There is no other mono MC cartridge like this available for this price. Bringing it from Japan was worth the wait. I liked it so much I may decide to seek out its stereo cousin for installation elsewhere.

For the price point this is a great addition to the toolbox for light to moderate use.

As I had enjoyed Graham Lewis' work with Omala as He Said Omala I decided this would be another strong side project by a truly awesome musician. The electronic style goes along with the musical vision of earlier albums by Lewis, namely Catch Supposes or his H.A.L.O. period. The sound of this album, with the strength of Andreas Karperyd who was a member of Omala He Said Omala, continues the path of ambient industrial music with spoken sung vocals throughout, but also the music takes on subtle jungle-esque, tribal influences, giving the whole album a more organic feel. The cover artwork gives a good idea of the music artificially stuffed animals. The music is richer than He Said Omala, and focuses on a multitude of ideas none of the tracks generally sound alike as they did on Catch Supposes. While the typical grizzly spoken sung vocals are back, they return in varied colors. The song structures are compelling and unique, and though keeping with the linear style of composing, the songs remain organic, not so 'cut and paste' in the likes of most electronic music, and while some songs could've used some shortening, the atmosphere the musicians were trying to give is recognizable, not misleading. The instrumentation itself is much stronger, as more gritty, distorted electronic loops are used in place of more conventional programming, and there are hints of a lot of different instruments and sounds, except for guitars, but they won't be missed when you get involved in music like this. And if you're as disappointed in Wire's latest album as I am, then you'll probably want to check out alternative side projects of the Wire collective, unless you already have.

I'm a pediatrician and I've used this stethoscope for many years. It's heavier than most, and it can hurt someone, so you have to be careful with the bell, don't let it swing around and hit anyone. But if you want to be able to hear heart and lung sounds well, I've never been more satisfied. I even use it on newborns. I'd rather make sure I hear a murmur than necessarily be able to localize it perfectly. They usually radiate pretty widely anyway so the precise location can be difficult to pinpoint even with a newborn stethoscope. Someone a patient actually first gave me this as a gift, maybe 15 years ago and I've just replaced them ever since. My last one can probably be fixed but I got this one anyway. I also like the color, it's more of an eggplant than the bright purple it looks like on here and it's easy to identify if you leave it sitting out somewhere as yours.

Let Freedom Reign is a great progression in Chrisette's musical career. I was blown away by the originality of her debut album and while her second was good, I felt that her creativity and songwriting ability were subjugated by the label thinking that pairing her with Ne-Yo would garner crossover appeal. Let Freedom Reign is a phenomenal album featuring strong songwriting lyrical arrangements and just plain good music. It ranks up there with Erykah Badu, Monica Fantasia's recent albums as a cohesive, consistent r b album. As a fan of strong singer songwriters, I am glad that Chrisette seemingly went with her gut and wrote 98 of the songs. Personally this is what draws me to her as an artist - her lyrics are thought-provoking and deep and while the album doesn't have a lot of hook-driven tracks, it is a grower because the more I listen to it, the better it gets. To those who listened once and are disappointed, please give it more of a chance to "marinate on your brain" by listening to it over time because this album is brilliant in construction content. Unfortunately for Chrisette she is not a priority of her label so hopefully good word of mouth about this album will cause more people to give her music a chance. She is one of few young r b female artists who are intelligent, write thoughtful lyrics and don't use sex to sell their image and I appreciate that and hope that more people discover her music. My standout tracks are I DON'T KNOW WHY, BUT I DO GOODBYE GAME I KNOW NOTHING IF NOBODY SANG ALONG I'M A STAR NUMBER ONE. IF NOBODY SANG ALONG is an especially honest track on which Chrisette debates selling out her image songs so that she can fit in with other artists vs. remaining true to herself.

My musical tastes have always been toward the adventurous, I like artists that are not afraid to take chances and twist things up a bit. Artists such as The Handsome Family, The Violent Femmes, Giant Sand, Beck, Benji Hughes and Captain Beefheart have been cornerstones of my music collection, not just for their bending genres but also because they transcend their respective ironies with a quality product. In other words their talents and drive make their iconoclasm work. Raymond Byron is such an artist, his influences are perhaps too numerous to list including everyone from Leonard Cohen to the Drive-By Truckers. He seems to take nothing for granted, each song is a small universe unto itself in the way it presents his fresh and sometimes satiric approach to a specific genre. Some songs such as the Cohenesque Whipporwill are played straight and display his immense songwriting skills. The song Stateline at a torturous 9 minutes has it's tongue planted firmly in it's cheek while it mutates and dissolves, it makes Townes Van Zandt sound like a perky pop star in comparison. Most songs are short and if not catchy, are quite creative. If you like the list of aforementioned artists for the reasons I have listed you will not be disappointed in this genre busting CD. Did I mention he is a great songwriter?

Miles Davis is known not to have had, at all times, the most warm-hearted demure where women were concerned, but I don't suppose any connection between his personal, religious or political life and the music he created. I never met Miles or Kahil El-Zabar, though have heard a fair amount of work by both. I purchased Renaissance of the Resistance expressly for two cuts, with the understanding that I was purchasing a CD, not an anthology of the man's music it's a CD - generally, we cannot join together and commune in music any more. We are obliged rather to buy it and consume it, often from unevenly edited CDs . This CD has some worthy and I think original contributions to a genre of The Music that is closer to the Cultural Nationalist inspired music, peotry and dance of the 60's and 70's than to Free Jazz or avante garde notwithstanding the titles of two cuts, "Ornette" and "Trane." These pieces are evocative of the two masters, John Coltrane and Ornette Coleman, but not intended as examples of the genre, I suspect I think we'd have seen quite different instrumentation had that been the intent . On the other hand, this music doesn't strike me as warmed-over Pharaoh or "Gary Bartz Ntu Troop all over" either. I found a fresh, clear meditative resonance between this music and some of what is done on his Big Cliff CD with Billy Bang on violin e.g. "For the Love of My Father" .

I also got the wire baskets and those are working out great they are heavy enough and the hooks fit in well. All in all it's great for medium duty tools and accessories, but I wouldn't want to have some of our heavier duty tools hanging up there.

Dan Fogelberg is was criminally overlooked as a songwriter and performer. Lots of platinum albums, and some of his best work is lesser known. Lot's of charted hits, but no Grammy awards. The sincerity of Jackson Brown with the versatility of the Eagles, the virtuosity of Joe Walsh, and immense talent in the vein of, not necessarily equal to, Crosby, Stills, Nash. I stand by all those words. Mr. Fogelberg was unfairly pigeon holed somewhat by love songs like Longer and other nonetheless quality songs. The essence of his worth is found on lesser knowns like Captured Angel, Home Free WOW as well as juggernaut production and composition and production records like Phoenix and The Innocent Age. It's never too late to invest in timeless music written by a rare soul. But you some Fogelberg and start enjoying!!

Bought this product about 8 months ago and at first it worked great, the meter was accurate all the time and didn't have to be recalibrated. Not sure why others are saying it changes every few days, I never had that issue. I would also point out I use pH meters frequently and teach science classes, so I know how to use them. The issue I am now having is that it suddenly began "spazzing out" for the lack of a better term whenever it's plugged into an outlet that has other electrical devices with high wattage running next to it. Not sure what caused this issue, it seemed to come out of nowhere, or how it can be fixed but i'm sure it has something to do with the electrical components because when i move it to an outlet with nothing else plugged in, or when I take it off the electrical connector, it works just fine. Overall I would say if you're buying a product like this for hydroponics then that means you're fairly serious about it and you're probably going to want to buy something that's better quality and will last longer. The product seems to be cheaply made, there was no calibration solution that came with it, and absolutely no technical support that I could ever find these are all bad signs of a company who makes cheap products . In the end you'll end up like me, ready to spend more money on a tool I've already purchased once.

I'm very much a guitar beginner so I'm still experimenting with some things to see what works best for me. I was actually looking only for the Wedgie pick holder and came across this sampler pack which includes the pick holder so I figured what the heck, let's give it a shot. I gotta tell you, after trying a bunch of these, what I thought I liked before is not what I like now. Because I was able to experiment with so many different materials and thicknesses, I have discovered I like a much thinner pick than what I was using. I'm also somewhat intrigued by the rubber picks, which I don't think I would have purchased on their own. A very unique sound on an acoustic.. Once I get a little better I think I'll be trying those a little more extensively. All of the Wedgie picks also have a contour on the body so you get a much more positive grip than with standard picks. The one thing that I have really gravitated toward is the flexx pick. I'm sure I will learn proper strumming technique, but in the meantime this little sucker makes it wayyy too easy to sound like I almost know what I'm doing.

I know, so why the three star rating if this is a great product. Well, since I am now a connoisseur of topical pain relief products then this one gets a three stars compared to another product that gets 5. So this will be a comparative review to this product Pain Block - Pain Relief Rub Cream 2 oz for Back, Joint, Knee, Shoulder, Rheumatoid Arthritis, Muscle, Neck, Fibromyalgia, Carpal Tunnel, Gout, Tendonitis, Plantar Fasciitis, Running Pain, Ganglion Cysts Speeds Healing 100 Guaranteed to Relieve Pain nbsp which I have used almost daily for about three months. So, Traumeel is a very good product, it worked well for my pain on my lower back and I had a little shoulder twinge that it also reduced the pain on. This ointment is a thick, very white cream like a zinc oxide, which I did not like. It is greasy and I could feel it on my body all day. This is the big downside for me. The odor was mild and non-offensive but if I got it on my clothes before it was rubbed in I could see it. With Pain block the pain relief was a little more on my back over time than Traumeel about two weeks of daily use before pain was completely gone with Traumeel the pain did not go away completely over the course of the month that I used it was good and definitely reduced the pain significantly but did not eliminate it completely . Pain Block got rid of my shoulder pain almost immediately where with Traumeel I used it first reduced the pain significantly on daily use for a week while it made it possible to do all normal and physical activity. All in all, Traumeel is a very good product that delivers and does what it says, for my money however, I will buy Pain Block again, but if I ran out of it this would be my substitute.

I am a part time mechanic. A citroen C5 came in the shop with a brake booster vacuum leak and no power brake. On inspection, the nipple that attaches the hose to the plastic intake manifold had broken off round causing the leak. The owner tried superglue, jb weld, epoxy - finally went to the dealer who suggested a new intake manifold for 1300. While working on his car, he did not even tell me the problem, but I found it and re-attached the nipple to the manifold with rapidfix adhesive, followed by two coatings of adhesive powder and adhesive combo. BOOM! PROBLEM SOLVED!! Air tight and as strong as the original unbroken nipple. Within 5 minutes it not only withstood the pressure of pressing on a new tight vacuum hose, but the owner in disbelief tried to break it off and ended up ripping the hose off with the nipple still attached firmly to the manifold! This product, used as per instructions, is one of the FEW products that actually work as good as the infomercials say it does. Trust me, buy it. Your very first use will likely pay for the value of the purchase.

Based on all the glorious reviews of the D'Addario NS Micro Clip-On Tuner, I decided to purchase the tuner. I already have a Snark SN-2 all instrument tuner that I really like, but I thought having a smaller tuner attached behind the headstock of my Telecaster would be better. Here are my impressions after using it for a couple of weeks. PROS First, the D'Addario NS Micro Clip-On Tuner is indeed small. After attaching it my guitar's headstock, it is almost unnoticeable from the front. Functionally, the tuner is very accurate. Green letter with a green bar on each side of the letter means it's tuned. Red means its either sharp or flat. There are red bars on either side of the red letter telling you how flat or sharp. After tuning the guitar with the NS Micro Clip-On Tuner, I checked my instrument with other tuners and it was spot on. CONS What I don't like about the tuner is the orientation of the letters A B C D E F G displayed. You are always reading them horizontally sideways . There is a button to change the orientation, but it's just sideways in reverse. You have to get used to reading the letters sideways or tilt your head sideways to read it properly. It may be no big deal to some people, but why not design it so that we can read it properly vertically . Another thing that I found annoying was that it was very finicky going from green to red and back to green again, without me even adjusting the guitar's tuning heads. Finally, attaching the tuner on the guitar's headstock will leave small indentations and marks on the wood, especially on the edges. Overall, I give the D'Addario NS Micro Clip-On Tuner 3 1 2 stars out of a possible 5. I have gone back to using my Snark SN-2, which I find much easier to use and read, and hasn't left any marks on my guitar's headstock in the 2 years that I've been using it. I would like to get a smaller tuner, and I'm looking at the nbsp Snark S1 Son of Snark Mini Clip-On Guitar and Bass Tuner .

I have tried a number of manufacturers for gloves that have a good fit and are suitable for wear in both a traditional and alternative processes darkroom. These are far and away the best in terms of fit and durability. They are ambidextrous and do not have an annoying tug from the thumb as some other brands do, which causes fatigue. They do not have any odor. My sense of touch remains very good enabling me to pick up large sheets of film without damage. The gloves protect my skin from chemicals and protect my film from fingerprints. I rinse, wipe dry while on , and reuse, discarding when they are stained, wet inside, or I don't want to cross contaminate. Another use for me has been to wear them in chilly weather while photographing. They permit very good finger dexterity, even when doubled up a 2-glove day . On very cold days I wear them inside leather gloves to set up and slip off the leather to make fine adjustments. If it's a 3-glove day, it's too cold for my equipment. I couldn't work without these gloves. The only gloves that are better are surgeon's gloves, which are prohibitively expensive. I also use these in the kitchen when handling raw meat and for household cleaning. I wear size medium. I have average to long fingers female , size 6.5 ring finger if that helps with determining size.

UPDATE FEB 2018 Speakers are now totally dead. No amount of fiddling with the switch in the back gets them to work again. My dad has Pioneer speakers from his college days that still work great, but these things pooped out after just over two years. It pains me to say this since they were great when they worked, but look elsewhere. There's definitely better durability for the price out there. ORIGINAL REVIEW It's now been over 2 years since I first bought this product. The first year or so was blissful. The monitors look good and seem well-put together but most importantly sound great. They are loud and crisp. The 3" is obviously lacking in the bass department, but that's to be expected in monitors this size at this price point. Had you asked me a year ago whether I would recommend these monitors I would have said definitely. HOWEVER! bum bum buuuuum About a year ago I was watching some video and noticed that audio was only coming out of one speaker. I checked all of my speaker wires and they were fine. I turned the speakers off and on, and nothing worked. Then as I was moving the main one the one with the volume knob magically it started working again. Fast forward a few months. This had been happening on and off the whole time. I finally narrowed it down to the speaker selection switch on the back, which lets you select which side the main speaker is on - left or right. If the audio went out in one speaker, I could jiggle that little switch and it would cut back on and off continuously until it hit just the right spot to keep both speakers working. I have to be careful not to touch or move the speakers though, because the slightest movement will make it cut out again. Sometimes it will cut in and out without me even touching them. I'm not sure why this is happening, and it might be an easy fix. I do plan on popping the main speaker open and inspecting the switch to see if there's anything that can be done. However I do know that these speakers stay hot, even when the power is "off". Meaning that if I turn the volume knob all the way down until it clicks into the off position and the light turns off, the back of the speaker where the amp is will stay hot continuously and never cool down unless I actually use the power off switch on the back of the speaker. BUT I hesitate to ever use that switch, because when I touch it it moves the speaker enough to make it cut out again!!! In summary, I really loved these speakers, and it's a shame that I have to give a less than stellar review, but I've listened to speakers older than I am that still work fine, and these things are giving up the ghost after just a year. Maybe you'll have a different experience, but if these speakers are all built the same I can't recommend them, as this is not a piece of equipment you should have to replace every year.

This is a terrific set of Chopin performances, but the pianist is not Dubravka Tomsic but Vlado Perlemuter. Perlemuter was a Nimbus artist when he made these recordings in the 1980s. I bought this set and found it excellent. However, I began to doubt that Tomsic was the pianist because all the sources I looked at showed her as only having recorded a few of Chopin's greatest hits, and not six full hours, as in this collection. I tried out the App Shazam, which can identify a specific musical piece or song AND its performer s by analyzing the wavelengths produced by the music. It's an amazing program. Anyway, when I analyzed the songs on this set with Shazam, I found that in words it attributed the performances to Tomsic, but there was the accompanying image showed the cover of a CD with Perlemuter playing Chopin. A little investigation brought me to this set of performances by Perelmuter nbsp Chopin Piano Works . The works played and the timings match up exactly with the Perlmuter set, which sells at a much higher price, 18.99, except that this set changes the order in which the pieces appear. Obviously, the Shazam database is dealing as best it can with confusing information, some of it pointing to Tosmic and some of it to the actual pianist, Perlemuter. So my rating averages a rating of five stars for the music and performance, and one star for the deceptive attribution of the performance.
