UPDATE Feb 2013 Do NOT let the bulb dry out. There is a piece of gauze resting against the bulb that needs to be kept wet. Let it dry our and you will be buying a replacement bulb. Original comment Have had the HM-200 for several years and it does a good job repeatable readings, ease of use . I give it a 5 because of quality and customer support. Based on quality price, the PH-200 is about the cheapest available, including units on ebay. Suddenly my HP-200 stopped working yesterday readings going wildly wrong mostly reading a 10.0 no matter the water tested . I called the company HM DIGITAL to find out why I could not calibrate it. The answer was that the sensor should be replaced every two years or so. He said there is a gel that gradually drys out. This is a wakeup because all ph meters use similar technology. Why this is important is that some ph meters do not have replaceable sensors. You can buy cheap meters but you take your chances on accuracy, mickey mouse calibration etc. If you are considering this or have one, I found a supplier that sells a replacement sensor at a reasonable price. the supplier is allwatercare com. in Atlanta. They also sell many different water testers and discount. I paid 38 for my replacement sensor while all other suppliers I checked wanted around 75 to 80. The company also has buffer solutions, cheap batteries etc. I think other companies just want to sell new units. Hope this helps. NOTE My wife said she had dropped the meter a while ago but there was no apparent damaged. The sensor is a glass bulb and although the unit continued to work but it may have damaged a seal allowing a leak, accelerating the gel loss.

This is a great song. I think the first track Bowie released from Blackstar--the nearly 10 minute long title track--is fascinating and musically adventurous. But its a bit emotionally obscure for me. Not so with Lazarus. Its beautiful and brooding. Bowies vocal is great. And the sax, especially the several minute long solo that builds to the song climax, is thrillingly unique. Bowie really is doing something new and different here, a marriage of rock and jazz unlike anything you or anyone else has heard before. Its Bowie at his bestfollowing his idiosyncratic muse. Not trying to surf the current of popular music or his own back catalog but instead swimming in some dark, mysterious sea that none of us even knew existed until he took us there.

I think a lot of people will ask, "What makes this thing worth so much when it's just made out of plywood?" Well, first, it's not plywood. It's made from a very precisely made laminate material. It's not the plywood you buy at the lumber yard. But what makes this better than the 50 laminate ukulele you can buy on the same page at Amazon? Is it just the name? Well, maybe a little. The cost of the material is only the smallest portion of the final price. It doesn't take much wood to build a ukulele. What costs money in musical instruments is precision manufacturing and quality control. That's the real difference between a 50 and a 300 ukulele. The difference is not only tone. Any well made laminate instrument will have about the same kind of tone. What you notice when you pick this up is 'intonation', that is, how well in tune the instrument is throughout its range. Intonation is the critical factor that makes a stringed instrument work correctly or not. Intonation is controlled by the precision with which the fret wires, nut, and bridge are placed, and all the components are fit together. On a small instrument like a soprano ukulele everything has to be exact to a very tiny amount. If anything off by just a couple of thousands of an inch then the notes will note be in tune with themselves. Getting that degree of perfection is expensive. It takes labor and time, and it takes careful quality control to make sure the bad ones don't get out the door. So that's why there are such huge ranges of price out there. Sure, sometimes you get lucky with a 50 instrument and it plays well. But more often you don't.

great product i bought one for myself and one for my brother so we could get into 3d printing together. i was a little worried about buying a kit because i have read some horror stories about kits, i was blown away by how good this kit was. we followed the directions and had no problems with assembly. we both have some knowledge of machinery and computers as well as tinkering which did help. as long as you have some technical ability and can follow directions it should be an easy build. the bed wasn't perfectly flat across the whole surface but i added a piece of glass to the surface and was able to make it dead nuts level. i checked the bed to nozzle height across the platform with a feeler gauge. and used my electronic calipers to check the measurements and levels over the rest of the build. i highly recommend having a precision measurement tool set to check build and print accuracy. i bought hachbox black pla filament and have not had a problem with printing. the filament that comes with the kit is not the best but does print kind of good. after tweaking the z axis and leveling the bed i was off and printing. the only print fails that i have had was due to bed adhesion and that was with crappy filament and no brim. over all the machine required almost no messing with to get it to print. total build time from box to first print was about 8 hours over 2 days. 4 day 1 and 4 day 2. first print was a robot directly on the print bed before i got the glass. it came out great but the camera or my camerawork cant get the details because of the shine. the space wrench came out good but i broke it almost immediately, my fault. the iris box was my third and most complicated print and it came out great. the 1up mushroom was my fourth print and was printed in different parts one print for the black and one print for the yellow. this printer does NOT come with a duel extruder . there are a lot of parts to this kit. it is a complicated machine. it comes with extra hardware so don't worry if you loose a nut or bolt. building this printer from a kit gave us a good idea about the machine and how it works. i highly recommend this kit to anyone who is not afraid to turn a wrench.

On his fourth release in his four year carrer, Ne-Yo decided to make a concept album. While concept albums, are nothing new, I can't recall an R B artist whose actually gone all out the way Ne-Yo has with Libra Scale. The album is full of the slow jams and music designed to make you feel good. The deluxe edition comes with three long form videos for the singles Beautiful Monster, Champagne Life and One In A Millon which all show Ne-Yo's creative side. After viewing the videos, it would have been nice to see the mini-movie that was oringally planned for the album. I can only think of one compliant with the album and that's the digipack, but other than that, Ne-Yo has produced another fine album and I can't wait for the tour. -- Track By Track-- 1. Champagne Life - The second single no explanation necessary. out of 2. Makin' A Movie - Ne-Yo has always provided some sexy slow jams on his albums and this is no expectation. out of 3. Know Your Name - This is another smooth slow that compliments the former track very well. I could easily see this video taking place after Champagne Life and before One In A Million. out of . 4. Telekinesis - Another sexy slow jam t completes a trilogy of slow jams. out of 5. Crazy Love feat. Fabolous - As a preview reviewer said Fab, spits a nice verse in this song and considering this is the sixth track that the two have done together, I'm sure that they could make an album together that would be very nice. out of . 6. One In A Million - The third single where Ne-Yo shows off his rapping skills. Much credit is given as Ne-Yo never goes into the territory of a singer trying to be a rapper. out of . 7. Genuine Only - Every Ne-Yo album has a filler song and this is the song on this album. I would have loved to have the slow jam Chillin on the album instead of this one. out of . 8. I inherited a very nice acoustic guitar A Taylor K14CE and wanted a strap for it, but I am deathly afraid of scratching it up, so the strap needed to have no metal or plastic parts. This was a bit cheaper than the Taylor straps, so I thought I'd give it a try. It is decently made, and easy to use, and feels very comfortable. A few caveats It doesn't fit the strap button on the back of the guitar without a bit of wrestling. However, it didn't advertise itself as fitting this specific guitar, which has I think a wider than average strap button because it doubles as a 1 4" plug, so I can't hold that against it. Also, I am not very tall, and this guitar is not a big one like a dreadnaught-style, but I still have to have the strap at its full extension in order to feel comfortable. So, if you are taller than 5'9 and or play a big-bodied guitar, you might want to reconsider. All in all, a fine strap and one I will probably have for as long as I have the guitar.

I am perplexed at the stellar reviews 'Extended Play' has been getting in the press. If you read them, they are almost word for word the same, so someone's doing a killer job with their PR. It's as if no one dares to speak badly about elder statesmen. And so those who assume I'm a fair-weather fan, I've purchased everything the band and solo artists have put out since 1973. I'd be lying if I said I wasn't curious about their four song Ep. What had a ten year absence brought after a 16 year one before that ? Well, not much - that's for sure. First off, any Fleetwood Mac album since 1987 has been a result of Lindsey Buckingham's solo work being taken and morphed into a Fleetwood Mac album - and it always shows. 'Extended Play' is no different. Three of the four are Buckingham tunes - and iffy ones at that. The one Stevie Nicks song is from 1973 - which isn't a shock. Almost all her material for the last 25 years has been pulled songs she wrote in the '70s. If they weren't good enough to record then.......guess what? Let's start with her "Without You". Cat Stevens should sue for plagiarism. The music and arrangement has "Peace Train" written all over it. and enough with finding these songs on YouTube, which she did on her last solo disk, and from a woman who claims not to own or use a computer. The criticism of Nicks' voice back in the '70s was somewhat unwarranted, but like a prophecy waiting to be fulfilled, she lived up to it long ago. Nicks' voice has been shot since the late '80's and for the life of me, the people who trip over themselves to sing ha! her praises is beyond me. The woman can no longer sing more than three notes and it is painful to listen to - mostly due to the nostalgia I'm feeling of what used to be. Her vocal limits can work when songs and vocals are arranged correctly. But I can think of a few instances in the last 20 years where that has worked. It didn't hurt that the harmony vocals helped save those few songs. For this outing, the harmonies are barely there....and they're all hers. Buckingham's "Sad Angel" is just half right. It's sad. I don't know it would have even worked as a solo song, but it doesn't pass muster as a band one. And speaking of solo stuff, his "It Takes Time" is just that. Seriously, if you're going to do a group record, then a song with just you and a piano isn't the one to present in this venue. Save it for your solo work. The only song that pseudo-works is the horribly titled "Miss Fantasy". Buckingham writes notes he can't possibly hit - and doesn't - but doesn't jigger the vocal arrangement. It is here that Nick's muted harmony on the chorus actually almost saves the song. What is sadder than the actual release is the band's assertion that this is some of their best work. Yikes. I get that they're 63 years old and up, but has their hearing gone too? I'm not sure whether to blame Buckingham or co-producer Mitchell Froom. Froom was a wiz with Crowded House, Forest for the Trees and Suzanne Vega. But he bombs out here. Or maybe this is the best he could do with which he had to work. I expect more from a band like this and not be expected to take with gratitude anything they push our way once every decade. They don't just get a pass. Either way - skip it. Save yourself 3.98.

This is perhaps the most underrated of all concerti--breathtakingly impassioned and heroic, and heart-renderingly lyrical, it leaves the listener exhausted. It's in a league with only Beethoven's 5th "Emperor," and Brahms's 1st, piano concerti. It combines their heroism with Schubertian lyricism. So, this concerto requires a performance which encompasses these traits, and only the present entry succeeds 100 . Each of the other Firkusny recordings of the work is also good, but only this performance has a total sense of abandon, utmost intensity. The recorded sound is fair-to-poor, with over-emphasis on the piano, and probably this live recording wouldn't have been issued at all if the performance didn't blow away all the competition as it does. This is a CD for music-lovers, but certainly not for audiophiles. The only other good or fairly good performances--worth maybe 2 to 3 stars--are Firkusny's others, especially the one with Szell, and the recordings by Kvapil and by Wuhrer, but none can touch this one. The performance by Kubelik of the 8th Symphony is also superb, but it has plenty of close competition, whereas the performance of the Piano Concerto doesn't.

Folks it is all about perspective. If you have never had great speakers these look and may even sound impressive to you. I had a pair of these for a couple of years. At first I was just using these for my big annual halloween party and a few stray DJ gigs that came my way. The bass was very bashful. I usually had to turn the bass way up to DJ for parties. The treble was a bit painful to listen to also. My ears are very sensitive, unlike most sound men who have made themselves nearly deaf from exposure to over cranked sound, I still hear very well. For Karaoke or leaving in some dingy club where you don't want to risk much of an investment these might be fine. A Year and a half ago I dumped these and switched to Mackie Thump powered speakers. The bass on the Mackies, which weigh half as much as these Pyles yet still sound waaaaaaay better, is amazing. The pyles are heavy, clunky and don't sound that great, but they are really cheap. For an amatuer user these might be fine. You might find the same quality for even less with Podium pro or Technical Pro speakers here on Amazon or on ebay. If you are serious about becoming a mobile DJ or mobile KJ look elsewhere. You need something that is light and mobile and sounds great these are heavy bulky and don't sound that great. Don't believe the specs given by any cheap manufacturer for amps or speakers ever!!!!! They all lie or exaggerate their specs. By changing the paramaters of how the specs are given, they can make them appear much better on paper than they actually are. For example they make a 200 watt usable RMS output spec out and just report that the speaker is 600 watts and often report frequency response to be 25 hz-20,000 hz when the usable freq responese within - 3db is actually more like 80hz to 17khz. Just don't ever fall in love with the specs of any cheap speaker or amplifier. There is no regulated standard of reporting sound equipment specs. So all manufacturers can pretty much say what they want, so why would they be honest? Go somewhere and listen to PA speakers, let your ears do the judging. That is the best education about sound equipment you can get. The more I think about it, take the specs reported by any speaker or amp manufacturer with a grain of salt, because it is so easy to exaggerate specs.

The battle rages on between these vacuum makers and each has pros and cons. I've owned two Dysons, an original yellow one and then the DC23 canister vac. While I realize you can't compare a canister directly with an upright it's what we have at the moment. After seeing all the ads and infomercials I decided to try a Shark and after finding a sale on refurbished units I decided to bite the bullet. It arrived in like new condition and I was very surprised, it beat my expectations on build quality. I almost expected flimsy Dirtdevil quality but the Shark is built like a tank with high quality plastic, tough as nails hose like the Dyson and pieces that clicked together with a firm, rock solid click. I'd say almost a tie her between the two brands. Time for testing. The lights on the wand and brush unit I also expected to perform like the old cheap Dirtdevil ones that wouldn't illuminate anything. Boy was I wrong, the lights are super bright and allowed me to see what I was vacuuming under furniture and in corners. Score one for Shark, zero for Dyson. Both are fairly portable with the Shark more so. The Dyson is one configuration, a canister so your always pulling it around, up and down stairs and the hose isn't stored anywhere so while carrying it around I tend to hook it on things. The Sharks lift away system is ingenius!! This allows for multiple configurations upright vac, pull it apart and it's almost like a canister vac where the main unit stays at the bottom top of the stairs and you have the freedom to move about. Or just separate the wand for quick cleaning. The rotator head with wheels also makes the Shark a breeze to move around on all floors. All in all the Shark takes up a little less space and stores better. Shark plus one, Dyson zero. Controls, the power button on the Dyson is on the unit which is a simple press with the foot, power on the Shark is on the handle. Sort of a tie depending on preference but I see why Shark put it on the handle as it's the main point of operation. The Dyson has a retractable power cord, the Shark does not. This is a problem if you want quick cleaning as the Shark you have to unravel the whole cord just to detach the wand. Plus one for the Dyson here. Both have quick emptying hands free bins so tie here. Cleaning. The power head on the Dyson is air powered so less breakable parts but we did wear one out and had to have it replaced. The Sharks powered head is belt driven so I imagine the belt would have to replaced at some point. Both are very powerful vacs. I tried two carpeted rooms with the Dyson going first and then the Shark second in one room and the second room I reversed the process with the Shark going first then the Dyson. Both picked up a little bit of extra dirt on the second pass so both vacs were leaving something behind and or loosening up dirt for the other to pick up. Tie here. Hose length. At first glance the Dyson appears to have a much longer hose but not so. The actual wand is three inches longer on the Dyson but when extending out the hose doesn't stretch and you end up trying and needing to pull the canister off the floor to reach cobwebs in high corners. The Shark while looking like the shorter hose very easily stretches to reach hard to reach places without pulling the whole unit along or off the floor. Conclusion While I still love Dyson the Shark has really surprised me with its innovative lift away system, build quality and other attention to details. If only they could figure out a retractable cord system this vac would be perfect. Four and a half stars!

I know it has a wider range than that but that is what I needed and what I can talk to. I am so very happy with this device every time I use it. I know that sounds like hyperbole but it is incredible to me that, for less than 100, I am able to know not guess what signal I am generating. Frontline' is a German melodic hard rock AOR Glam metal band that emerged during the early-mid 90s and The state of rock' is their debut album. Along with other Eurpean acts that debuted at the same time such as Fair Warning' and Lionsheart', Frontiline' was mainly addressed to the Japanese market which would continue to demonstrate demand for the particular genre of bands. The group consists of lead singer Stephan Kaemmer, guitarist Robby Boebel who also plays keyboards on the album , bass player Thomas Riess and drummer Stephan Bayerlein. Undoubtedly we are looking at one of the greatest AOR Hard rock albums of the 90s for a number of reasons Being recorded during the 90s the album naturally demonstrates more advanced sound production than albums recorded during the 80s. In addition the songwriting is based on incredible melodic guitar lines including impressive leads and shredding solos, evidently outweighing the keyboards. Equally important is the fact that despite coming from Germany, Frontline' follows the American AOR melodic Hard rock approach as the material is heavy and melodic but not harsh or fast. In addition their lead singer also demonstrates very melodic and clean vocals and shines wonderfully when accompanied by the backing vocals. Every song on this album is very well practiced and clearly differentiated from the rest, therefore avoiding that repetition of melodies that many albums suffer from. Favorite tracks include the climactic Heaven Knows', the thumping Endless', the super melodic Another love' the aggressive Dangerous game' and Victim of madness'. Frontline' would come to release more albums through the 90s and 00s and reform in 2009 under the band name State of Rock' with Tony Mills as their new front man.

Theres no delay, new or used, that comes close to this at this price - or even two, three, or four times the price. A couple of years ago I got back in to playing, and in the past year, Ive been through close to a dozen delays trying to find one s that inspired me. Cool tones abound, but I often spent too much time twiddling knobs and digging through presets rather than actually losing myself in playing. For 35 same price as the power supply for one of the delays mentioned above , the Yellow Fall changed all that. Within minutes of plugging in, Id going from programming my pedal to actually playing again. Inspiration, instinct, interaction, immediacy - all of the things I look for in a good piece of gear. And this certainly falls into that category. That said, its not a one trick pony either. The control set is elementary, the building blocks of what you need for straight head classic surf, rock, and blues tones, up through more experiment and ambient textures are all right in front of you. Its less than a cost of a movie, and likely more fun. Haven't had it as long as some of my other pieces of gear, but it looks, sounds, and feels well build. No loose parts or connections. Surprisingly nice packaging for such a low dollar item. Cant lose.

I'm really impressed with the build quality and function of this device. The battery lasted almost a year running a humidifier in my basement. Never the slightest problem. This device is extremely useful for signaling high low humidity or temperature. Note that as a battery powered device it's not intended to be constantly polled for current conditions. It does report temp and humidity, but its primary function is to "alarm" on out of bounds conditions. In my use this device "wakes up" and tells vera that the basement humidity is above 55 . Vera turns on the dehumidifier. When humidity reaches 45 , this device wakes up again and reports to Vera. Why four stars? Can't beat the price so it was perfect for lighting our dark closet but I would never recommend it for more decorative applications for three major reasons 1 The 3000K color temperature visibly shifts along the run that means it will look whiter in one section and more yellow in another 2 The end of the 16' run is dimmer due to a voltage drop in the run from the plug in driver to the further end that means it will look dimmer on the end of the long run 3 Although the switch dimmer feature is nice, it only turns off the LED tape light which leaves the plug in driver "hot" at all times unless turned off from anther switching device. I use better brand tape light all through my kitchen which is much more consistent and reliable and more expensive of course from Nora Lighting. I didn't see the point of spending that much money on a small closet so this was ideal. With tape light and other LED products you really do get what you pay for. I'd only recommend it for closets and utility ares. For decorative runs throughout my home, I turn to the good stuff. Do it once, do it right.

This is a heavy duty well made vice. I bought the 7 inch which is adequate for nearly all my needs. I love the quick release and that it has a pop-up dog for use with the bench dog system on my assembly table. I did need to grind a bit not much on the back of the casting so it would set true and square when I mounted it to my bench as it's simply raw casting there and not machined in any way, probably helps keep the price down. Also took a bit of shimming the mounts where it bolts to the underside to keep the faces square to my bench, but I expect that most such vises would take a bit of careful fitting. Overall the casting and machining on this vise appear top-notch. I think it's a really good deal for a small or hobby wood shop. I used through carriage bolts countersunk and plugged flush on the top of my bench as I felt this stronger than just lag bolts from the bottom. It's a bit more work, but I feel worth the effort so it won't shift or loosen over the years due to normal humidity and stresses. This unit works like a charm.

We have been playing with this device attached to our live PA system for a while and people in the scene ask us for our "punched" sound. Well this is the secret. This device is a mystery black box that doubles the value of your equipment by flicking a switch. Apparently the generic term for this kind of device is "aural exciter" if you want to look for alternatives . This device enhance the harmonic frequencies from the signal that is entering and correct the phase of conflicting harmonics. It avoids conflicting signals to cancel out resulting in a lowered fidelity of sound for higher and lower registers. Describing it in qualitative terms, the bass will sound loud and contained in the bass low register without invading other instrument sound frequencies. The guitar will shimmer without stepping in the opaque and dark warm register of the bass instruments, the drum will sound snappy and defined and the voices very clear without been muddled by the other instruments. It feels like it give a protagonist role to each instrument. Everything will sound like a studio recording. And of course you can regulate the amount of enhancement you want to apply if you find it too synthetic sounding. Each channel has two knobs one for "Lo contour" that seems to work like bass boost an a "Process" that controls the amount of FX and presence.

I used this for 3D printing and up until now was using the stock i3 heating element for the bed that came with my printer kit. Let me tell you this makes a huge difference! It was taking anywhere from 20-30 minutes to heat up the bed. Take extra long if my AC was on and it was cool in the house. With this it doesnt matter. It goes from 0-80C in about 3-4 minutes. This thing is FAST! And not just fast but it also maintains its temperature extremely well. My other bed would fluctuate when doing long prints. Not this. It stays exact what you set it at. You just need to make sure to also buy a good quality thermistor to monitor temperature and some 3M silicone adhesive to bond this to a plate. I am extremely happy with this upgrade. So far it has made printing a ton easier since I no long have to wait just to get it started. I wish I had bought this sooner. But better late than never I guess. Anyway if you plan on using this with a 3D printer, it is definitely a wise investment. Make sure to get a new plate too since you have to basically permanently bond this to it. I kept my old stock plate just in case something goes wrong I can still keep printing. Plates are cheap anyway. Well hope this review helps some.

I bought a Suzuki Folk Master Harmonica, Model 1072, to replace my Hohner Big River Harp. I bought the Big River back when they cost 12. It's old and corroded but a new Big River costs twice as much now. I figured I'd just buy 2, non Hohner, harmonicas for about what one new Big River costs. I bought the Suzuki Folk Master and a Swan Harmonica for just shy of 15 each, here on Amazon. Now I can compare the 3 of them. The first thing I noticed about the Folk Master was the sound. Right out of the box the notes were crystal clear and easy to play. Better than the old Big River. Better and louder than the Swan. It is a sweet harp to play! I'm a tinkerer. I like to tear things apart, fiddle with the insides, and put them back together. I just had to take a look at the guts of the Folk Master. Disassembling a harmonica is important for cleaning and adjusting the reeds. This harmonica was easy enough to disassemble. The reeds need no adjustment. But I found something troubling. Of the 7 screws holding the reed plates to the comb, one was stripped. It wasn't holding anything. While the reed plates are of high quality, the screws are not. I figured 6 screws were good enough since my old Big River only has 3. Low quality materials are not a good sign. I reassembled the Folk Master and discovered another troubling sign. The tolerances are sloppy. When I reassemble the Big River and Swan, the screw holes for the cover plates line up with the reed plates and comb to hold everything in place not so with the Folk Master. The holes did not line up and had to be shoved to fit. The cover plates must fit into a groove on the reed plates. If they don't, the cover plates will slip over the holes on the comb. It was a real wrestling match to get the cover plates hooked into the reed plates, shove the plates over so the screw holes will align, and fiddle with the screws and nuts to get the whole mess to fit together. It took me several tries before I got it right. Since it still played beautifully, I was going to give it 4 out of 5 stars then something bad happened. I was tapping the spit out of the harmonica by holding it by the back, placing my index finger along the comb, and tapping it into my other hand. When I went to play it, I found it stuck to my finger. The cover plate had sliced into my finger and was hanging in the cut! This is a design flaw! The cover plates on most harmonicas are made of thin metal, like a dull knife, and the stamping process can make what is known as a wire edge. These can be very sharp. The Big River, from Germany, and the Swan, from China, are both designed in such a way so you cannot touch the edges of the cover plates. There is no way you can get cut with either of these harmonicas once they are assembled. I tore the Folk Master apart and filed down the cover plates to remove the wire edge. Now they are like dull knife blades. Just a little safer. Musical instruments should not draw blood! I had high hopes for the sweet playing Suzuki Folk Master but because of the low quality materials, sloppy tolerances, and hazardous design flaws, I have to declare it unacceptable and give it only one star.

I love Tim Hecker's work. I love how you can disappear inside it, immerse yourself. I love how nothing ever seems to start or end it just is. But I also love to distance myself from its mesmerizing spell and marvel at the sound's texture. I try to deconstruct how each track is arranged, how the album is put together. Tim's ear and taste is as good as Aphex Twin's or DJ Shadow's, the two giants who predate him and who, at surface, are so different. Hecker is meticulous in how he wants his albums to sound and works hard to make them interesting. They are ever changing and always satisfying. Ravedeath 1972 is a wonderful work of art and a rich feast for your ears. For anyone interested in ambitious ambient electronica, this is a fantastic place to begin. The lines between "classical" and "popular" blur. Give yourself room to discover Hecker's latest opus. You will not be disappointed.

While many argue that Nevermind is either overrated or underrated, I find it to be both. It's overrated since I disagree with many calling it the greatest album of all-time or the greatest alt-rock album ever. It's not the greatest album ever or even the greatest alt-rock album. Albums like the Pixies' Doolittle, R.E.M.'s Murmur, The Clash's London Calling, and Pavement's Slanted And Enchanted are all better rock albums than Nevermind. Why VH1 called it the second greatest album of rock roll is a mystery to me. However, Nevermind is underrated since many seem to ignore how talented and great Nirvana was. Yeah, they sold millions of records and broke into the mainstream, but they never sold out or changed their sound to become popular. I seriously doubt they wanted to become as big as they did. They did not "rip-off" the Pixies, Husker Du, or Sonic Youth. Rather, they took those influences and created a sound that was their own. There is not one weak track on Nevermind. "Something In The Way," "Come As You Are," "In Bloom," "Drain You," etc. are all great, brilliant songs. The only thing that might take away from the album is that fact that their singles "Smells Like Teen Spirit," "Lithium," "Come As You Are," and "In Bloom" were seriously overplayed on MTV and radio. But is that Nirvana's fault? Nope. Nevermind is a very, very, very good album. It deserves a listen from rock fans everywhere. It is one of those albums where you won't skip any tracks. Nirvana had their own, unique sound and were very original and creative. The greatest album ever? Nope. An excellent and classic album? Yes.

I would purchase again, I plan on re-reviewing in a couple months to see if the unit still holds together.

This model seems "inspired" by Fender's Jaco Pastorius model. I found mine by accident while shopping at a Guitar Center. I asked if there were any fretless basses on the floor wall I could try. There were none but they had just gotten in this one to sell as used and were in the process of cleaning it up and restringing. Played wonderfully, and was offered at a substantial discount from MRSP, so bought it on the spot Had to wait two weeks for police clearance to make sure it wasn't reported stolen. After living with it for nine months, it is one of my two primary basses. I have a vintage Fender Jazz Bass and though this one is called a "Jazz Bass," it's neck and fretboard are not the same. This one is noticeably wider toward the body than my Fender. My hand tells me the fretboard radius is larger too feels "flatter," again more noticeable closer to the body. Neither are negatives, per se, but different than a Jazz Bass. Perhaps the neck fretboard specs are taken from the Jaco model versus a standard Jazz. I had minor quibbles with the pots, control plate and jack noisy pots, screws and nuts kept coming loose but a little DIY and those issues are gone. This was strung with flatwounds by Guitar Center. I don't normally use flats, so it felt and sounded a bit different than my other basses. I recently put some roundwounds to try for a more trebly sound but results were less than what I'd hoped so will be putting flats back on. Flats will mean it doesn't have that cutting, piano-ish tone my Fenders with roundwounds get, but I can compensate somewhat by amp settings. The flats also just "feel better" on this bass. I don't think this bass would be a good choice for a beginner's bass but is a great value for someone more experienced who's wanting to explore and experiment with fretless.

Edit Just chiming in a year later here. I realize now what I want is a vintage P-bass tone, not a really midrange heavy "modern" pickup. The pickup should only be bought if you plan on coupling it to some sort of preamp because it just sounds really weak without it. The pickup also doesn't react to new strings well because of too many hi-mids that "honky" sort of tone . Sorry to burst anyone's bubble, but if you guys want a good EMG bass pickup then get either the Active P or an EMG PX. I have been playing bass since 2007, this is my first replacement pickup so far that I have put in my old Yamaha RBX260. It is very clean sounding, and it can pull off a number of sounds provided you mess with the EQ on your amplifier. This pickup has a crap ton of bass boom to it on my E string,but the sound is not as punchy as the active EMGs. However, I can get the "Enter Sandman" tone if I boost my high mids and the James Jamerson kind of thump if I use a mute, boost my bass, and cut my mids. Flat it has a nice sound for slapping. The sound also varies greatly from amp to amp. I use a Behirnger BXL1800A and an old Ibanez Soundwave 20 watt. It sounds brighter and clearer through the Behringer thanks to the aluminum cone, and warmer in the Ibanez amp because of the paper cone. It's not the dark tone I've been looking for but I'm sure if I switch to flats and replace the stock pots it will change indefinitely.

I've had this speaker for over 7 months now. I use it every now and then, not as often as I would like, but I believe I am now qualified to write a review on it. Yes, it has held up, which is probably one of the primary concerns for many people looking at a Dj speaker under 200. It has a pretty decent construction and I can't see any scuffs which I may have caused the unit to receive in various transports. The sound quality is quite good. It goes pretty loud compared to some of the "budget friendly" Dj speakers and their not-so-acclaimed companies I have used before. Bass is pretty decent it gets quite low in Hz for a speaker and can be cranked up in SPL on the unit itself. I have actually set the speaker on my floor with the bass cranked up and had some lighter objects fall off my shelves. However, the extra-low frequencies don't travel that far or give the chest thump from a distance as many people see as a drawback. I would just like to remind the purchaser s of this product that it is a speaker, not a subwoofer. I think this would be greatly complimented by a Rockville RBG18FA to help with the low end, but the speaker should not really be knocked off points for that. The weight is somewhat on the beefier side and should be taken into account by potential buyers. I believe you actually get much more than what you pay for with this unit. It is half the cost of many similar entry level units and offers much more. For less than 200, this speaker lessens the economic gap many starting Djs have to get over to get out there and do their thing. I give it 5 stars.

I unfortunately don't have a lot of Hannah Fury's music on my iPod, but this is, hands down, my favorite. From literally the first moment I heard the opening notes of the song, I knew I was going to love it, and I was right. The song itself is calm, low and dark, a crooning lullaby that speaks of brutal truth and I confess, I listen to it sometimes when I go to sleep ...but what truly stands out about this song is the AMAZING lyrics "Girls that glitter love the dark We lace it through our bitter, black little hearts And then we feign surprise when we see That those we've loved to love Have seeped into our poisoned blood..." Knit it through, thread it, sew it to unsuspecting flesh They won't regret it, or so they say And those you have loved have snagged on your thorny veins But they don't regret it, or so you say... We see love trying desperately to flee But you don't ever have to fear Just break its wings and it will never disappear..." ---To me, they have a Gothic undertone to them, but they can be interpreted in so many different ways, all of them true. GET THIS SONG. What's not to love about it?

They've stopped coming. They used to come four times a day, to feed me, to keep me furnished, sometimes to test me. It was horrible some days, especially when they slipped up and I started hurting. But they used to come. Now the room's dirty beyond belief, all the electronics have stopped working, and the hurting won't stop. Why aren't they coming? It's getting dark, too. Did they cut the power? Or is that me blacking out? Or the burning fluid getting in my eyes? I don't like the dark it scares me, it has all the feelings that hurt me in it. I want them to bring back the lights. I've tried the door a few times. I thought the burning stuff would eat through it if they had turned the power off, but it only hurt me. I can't force the lock. There's no other way out. Everything's decaying around me, and I'm feeling worse, and I feel bad about that and I'm hurting myself more and more. It must be really bad out there I'm hurting more than my own emotions could make me. I used to burn every so often, but now I do it everyday. My skin feels like it's peeling off, and I can't even scream sometimes, because my throat's so clogged up. I need them to come back. I need them to make the burning stop. And my head hurts. Not like the burning stuff makes it hurt, but like there's a vice on it. Something's squeezing my head in, taking my memories. It's like there's a giant sponge in the walls, eating away at everything I am. But that's not possible. They said the things they put in the walls stop me from hurting. Why can't I remember my name? They told me they're always watching. They know I'm suffering. In the past, when I hurt, they came and saved me. But now, I just hurt alone. It's like they threw me away because I'm not good enough. John. You brought me here. Please, get me out. Help.

For the money, this thing is totally playable. You're never going to be blown away with the quality, it's cheap and it shows as you live with it, but with the right ear for pitch and trumpet chops for range, it speaks very well. I've actually had a lot of fun and raised some eyebrows with the horn. Works well with trumpet mutes, and a Harmon mute is amazing with this little guy. I spent a lot of time getting the slide how I like, I'm picky about bone slides, and with much effort I got to a workable place with the slide. But not exactly thrilled either, there's a cheap and non-glossy feel to it no matter what product I use thouroughly cleaned between switching slide treatments of course . Slide-O-Mix has worked best. These soprano bones slide trumpets are a challenge, trumpet players will find slide work foreign and bone players will find range difficult initially. Either player can make it work though, and the slide vs valves makes for a very interesting voice in the trumpet register. An ear for pitch is critical, and that's the nature of the beast, not an issue with this model.

This is simply one of the best instruments I've ever bought. The best thing is that you have the option of using bass guitar fingering or Simandl fingering, whatever works best. Plus you don't have to bother learning thumb position. If you're coming from a fretless bass guitar, you can kind of wail on this thing right away you're sure to hit some wrong notes for a while, but it's a lot easier than transitioning to a 41" scale length. I know, I have owned both this and the standard NS NXT upright. It's also a little more portable. Another great thing about it is the pickup mode switch, which basically gives you two useful sounds gut-string upright or electric somewhere between Jaco and Chris Squire . With the switch in the "gut-string" mode, it sounds absolutely amazing when bowed -- sort of like an orchestral upright on steroids. I don't find the included strap comfortable, so I bought the fancier of the two stands NS offers, which I like a lot. The other stand is lighter, but it's a tripod design with legs that get in the way. I haven't tried the end pin stand yet, but I'm sure I will at some point. The stand I have is pretty heavy. One note With the high output impedance of the passive piezo pickups, this bass sounds a lot better feeding an input impedance of at least a few megaohms. It sounds really good with my Phil Jones amp, which is 4 Mohm input impedance. If you're going to use it with a standard amp, I recommend getting an HPF-Pre to go with it.

Kitchen Basics used to be the most delicious chicken stock. When it got bought by McCormick in 2015, it seemed a little lighter, but still retained its essential character. With the newest box, the stock is now completely different. It smelled predominantly of celery and had lost all of its rich orangy yellow color. Looking at the ingredients, mushrooms have been completely eliminated, and there are now more salts both sodium and potassium than it used to have. Original ingredients Chicken stock, chicken flavor, honey, vegetable stocks carrot, onion, mushroom, celery , bay, thyme, pepper. 2015 McCormick ingredients Chicken Stock, Vegetable Stock Carrot, Onion, Mushrooms, And Celery , Natural Chicken Flavor, Honey, Salt, NATURAL FLAVOR e.g. yeast extract containing MSG , And Spice And Herbs Black Pepper, Bay Leaf, And Thyme . 2016 McCormick ingredients chicken stock, natural chicken flavor, cooked vegetables celery, carrot, onion , honey, spice and herbs black pepper, bayleaf, thyme , and natural flavor. To go with these changes, the boxes have been getting progressively lighter, as the stock becomes progressively watered down, so that the color of the stock is now completely different. The new box is on the left in the attached photo. Stocks appear darker in the two cup measuring cup than the one cup, so the difference in color is even more dramatic than appears in the photo . I went back to my store to return the unopened carton, and the manager found 10 remaining boxes in the back room of the older version, and I bought all of them. I plan to write to McCormick as they said to contact them if you are dissatisfied with the product. If enough people complain, maybe they would consider bringing back the older formula. For now, it is clear that these stocks are NOT worth the extra cost that I used to be willing to pay for Kitchen Basics.

nbsp I got this mini float valve to install inside my Keurig water tank so it would automatically refill itself. This little valve works perfectly. The valve itself needs a 5 8" hole through which the mounting shaft fits. I drilled the hole easily with a step drill similar to the nbsp Vastar Titanium Step Drill Cone Drill Bits, High Speed Steel 10 Steps 1 4" to 1-3 8" . They work great when cutting holes in thin plastic as they gradually cut the hole with very little stress on the plastic. You tighten the valve body into place with a plastic nut that compresses a silicon washer on the inside and a hard plastic washer on the outside. This leaves a threaded hole exposed in the valve. After wrapping a generous length of teflon tape over the fine threads of the 90-degree elbow I had to do this three times, increasing the teflon each time until the threads no longer leaked you screw the elbow into the valve body. I stopped when the elbow faced rearward. At this point you can insert standard 1 4" polyethylene tubing into the elbow until it reaches its stop. If you need to adapt to 3 8" tubing, you'll still need a short length of 1 4" tubing to use the included 1 4" to 3 8" adapter coupling. I didn't need that. You really only need 1 4" tubing to feed the tank as you're only replenishing the 10 ounces of water used to make a single cup of coffee after each brewing. A trickle is all you need. But the valve refills the tank almost immediately. I'd highly recommend having a main valve further down the line before the float valve. This allows you to turn the water off if you need to clean or decalcify the coffee maker or otherwise stop the water flow. I had already installed a 6.43 plastic 1 4" to 1 4" ball valve nbsp John Guest PPSV040808WP Single Straight Shut-Off Valve, 1 4" Tube OD x 1 4" nbsp for an earlier coffee maker that I had plumbed with a different float valve. The water comes from our reverse osmosis system that supplies water to the sink and refrigerator icemaker. This valve works well because as you can see from the pictures, the valve has to come through the top of the tank lid. This means the float arm needs to articulate. A stainless screw allows the plastic knee joint to adjust the float to suit the desired water level. The float fits easily inside my particular Keurig with about 1 2" to spare on either side of the float as it rides up and down on the water surface. The indicated dimensions of 45mm diameter x 74mm long appear to be accurate. I did put a piece of Scotch tape on the lid to hold the lid onto the tank. If someone were to remove the lid, it would bring the valve out with it, the float would lower and water would start flowing. Best to lock the lid onto the tank with a piece of tape. My previous mini-float valve worked flawlessly for more than ten years. I only retired it because the coffeemaker failed and its design called for a float that mounted on the back of the tank. This valve has to come in from the top. The valves are very simple with no parts to corrode. I hope to get the same service from this valve.

This is not the first PCB film I've used, but it is, by far, the worst. It's actually unusable because of the huge inconsistencies in thickness, meaning there are areas with so little coverage they are effectively exposed, even if not exposed, even on very coarse trace boards. No matter how development was conducted, this film was too inconsistent to be used. Areas which received zero exposure but were from thicker parts of the film remained in place even after massive over development, whereas thinner parts of the film were unable to be exposed enough to be able to survive even minimal development. Don't waste your time - you might get lucky and get a good roll like some folks here seem to have received, but the roll I received was clearly a reject by any reasonable measure. There are alternatives. I'll be returning this roll, as I've used less than 30cm and it is clearly not fit for purpose.

So far I really love this amp!! Great verity of sounds and a lot of fun to play with!! It has a surprisingly full sound for a small 20 watt cab. I really want to give this amp 5 stars, but I have to take off for the interface being a little awkward. This is a digital amp that makes no attempt to work the way you expect a classic amp to work, so be ready to learn something that is entirely new. Flipping through the factory presets easily gives you a wide range of amazing sounds. There are 24 presets that cover just about everything you might look for. If you like to make minor tweaks to personalize your sound, you may be a little frustrated by the interface. For example, recalling a preset causes all the knobs to change to an internal position that you cannot see they are not motorized . If you then adjust any knob at all, it instantly switch's from the hidden position to the physical position, which may result in a huge and unexpected change. If you plan to install the free Fuse software and connect a computer using the included USB cable, then all of my interface complaints go away. The computer interface is beautiful, very logical and smooth to use. You can control the entire amp from the computer, make any change you want, including settings impossible to access from the amp's limited interface.

The product itself seemed to work good. I used it one day in several areas and put aside following the manufacturer instructions how to shut the valves, etc. When I wanted to use it next time, the foam wouldn't show up. So I started my investigation by taking apart all the components of the hose, in search for dried foam. At the same time I cleaned everything just in case with foam cleaner acetone . I didn't find anything but the hose still wouldn't work. I bought this specifically to use with an iPad 4. The first time I plugged it in using the official Lightning Camera Connection Cable, nothing happened. I had seen the UCA222 work in video's directly with iPads so I was worried I had a dud UCA222. I plugged the UCA222 into my mac and it came right on. After doing a quick google I saw that another person had a similar problem and simply rebooting the iPad fixed it. I unplugged the UCA222 from the mac and plugged it into the iPad and it came right now without a reboot. Now that it was working I was able to connect a KP2 that had my iPhone plugged into it... The sound was loud and clear. I used the headphone monitor on the UCA222 and it worked perfect. Spent the rest of the night having a blast recording in Loopy. I was very happy with the setup... easy, no power strips, no tangle of cables... just easy fun. Build quality is what you would expect for 30... the switches and RCA Jacks feel VERY flimsy. Plugging the headphones into the headphone jack feels like you are connecting to a jack that is simply soldered to a PCB with NO other support... so I expect that to be a point of failure at some point. I am pretty easy on my gear, so hopefully I will get good use before it dies. Oh, also it needs NO drivers on mac... it just shows up as a USB interface and worked perfect. I will probably use it with laptops in the future. I have not tried the digital I O, at all so not sure how that works.

For the price, this neck makes a very good replacement neck. It requires a little reshaping to fit nicely into Telecaster bodies, but being a builder that is easy for me to take care of. The neck is straight, Tuss rod works nicely, frets are even, no string buzz and frets are small easier to play than a jumbo size fret. The frets need a little filing on the ends to make it smother to move up and down the neck, but again easy task to do. I did replace the nut with a tuscon nut and cut it for best height on all strings. The neck body is 1 16 of an inch smaller at the nut, but that is not a problem. Standard is 1 and 11 16 and it measures 1 10 16 of an inch or 1 5 8 of an inch. With a little shaping at the base, a new nut, and a good finish it will look and feel like one of Leo Fender's early Telecaster models. And that aint bad at all. I will buy more of them. Guess you could say I am impressed with the quality for the price.

"Whisperings Solo Piano Volume 2" is the third compilation from the Whisperings Solo Piano Radio group of artists the second was a Christmas album , and what an inspiring collection it is! Sixteen artists are represented and all of the music was recorded and mastered at Piano Haven by Joe Bongiorno, giving the album a much more consistent and even sound than that of most compilations. The ebb and flow of moods and styles is excellent despite the variety of artists - no small feat there. All of the artists are different from "Volume 1" except for David Nevue the founder of Whisperings and Joe Bongiorno and they certainly deserve to be on all of the Whisperings albums for their amazing dedication and efforts to make Whisperings the success it is. I have watched Whisperings grow from a very small group of pianists to the current roster of 276 artists from around the world. David Nevue visualized and created a community of artists and a distinctive brand of music and what a joy it has been to see it succeed and continue to grow! The spirit of friendship and mutual support makes Whisperings much more than the sum of its parts. Back to the music! There are no weak tracks on "Whisperings Volume 2," and despite the title, some of the music is quite upbeat and even exuberant - and always a joy to listen to! Dont get me wrong - there are several soft and soothing pieces, but there are also some lively ones. Its an exceptional blend and the album is a shoo-in for my Favorite Albums of 2014 list. Very highly and enthusiastically recommended!

This hat makes a HUGE difference in responsiveness. I had the standard VH-11 hi-hat trigger for my Roland TD-30KV kit, but that hat never really did what I wanted it to. It was kind of... odd. I bought this trigger set, and now it's much much more like playing a real hi-hat! A lot of people are wondering if this is a one-piece hat or two-piece. It is a two-piece clamshell system. Bottom hat and top hat are included. You put it on your stand like regular hi-hats, and you connect the two with a small tether cable in between them. It is kept from rotating by putting a collar and tether system that attaches the bottom hat to the hi-hat stand, which keeps the rig from spinning around and around. Calibrating these is as easy as pie, too. Once you've selected the trigger type in your drum brain, you open the clutch and let the pads sit on each other. Then you hit calibrate, and it automatically does all the work! You will LOVE this trigger if you are using Roland Vdrums, and if you are tired of your hi-hats never being juuuuust right.

I have never had a glue hold on any shoe repair until now. I squeezed the barge glue in to it, wiped off the excess, and pinned it under a weighted cardboard box. The bond to the leather and rubber is perfect, just as it came from the shoe manufacturer. Great product, highly recommend for shoe repair and anything else.

I guess that's a pun of some kind. Anyway, about 30 years ago I went to a lecture about colon cancer at the company health club, and decided that wasn't for me. The MD presenting said an easy way to avoid it was to eat All-Bran for breakfast, and since then I've only missed maybe 10 days. All-Bran is hard to find in supermarkets, almost impossible to find in restaurants, so the subscription is very convenient. It may taste like cardboard vanilla flavored almond milk helps , but it does the job, and my colonoscopies are consistently boring. Update 4 12 15 This is no longer available in my area, and was a product of Mexico when last purchsed -- even the box was of poor quality. And, as others have noted, it's wicked expensive and possibly contaminated and of lesser quality these days when ordered online. I have complained regularly, both in my local stores "sorry, we can't get it" , and to Kellogs "we've changed manufacturers, should be available soon" . The net result of my complaints has been one coupon from Kellogs for 1.25. Looking at alternatives that others have recommended as well as Citrucel, or Life Extension fiber supplements. Very disappointed in Kellogs, who have a lot of work to do to regain my business and trust. Excellent website at rating many breakfast cereals read the ingredients and look at the total grams of fiber soluble and insoluble , calories, fat and sugar. I'm abandoning All-Bran until Kellog's gets their act together. Both Post and General Mills have what appear to be viable alternatives. In case Amazon censors URLs, Google "How does your cereal rate?".

This album was made in 1958, containing some of my favorite Monk tunes, all of which was played with the energy and musicianship that would have done a live performance credit. Personnel on this session were Monk and Blakey on piano and drums, respectively, Bill Hardman on trumpet, the great Johnny Griffin on tenor saxophone and Spanky DeBrest on bass. This is the original version of the album in 1999 a reissue album added alternate takes of Evidence, Blue Monk and I Mean You. Personally, I prefer this original version, and sincerely believe that the renditions on this album of what are some of Monk's best compositions are among the best ever recorded in a studio. If you are strictly a Jazz Messenger fan, this is a good addition to your collection. If you are a Monk fan I recommend grabbing it.

I don't recommend this Roomba unless you are looking to vacuum only 325 square feet of space at one time, and only for one hour. I read the whole description on this Roomba and there was nothing that said this little robot vacuum only vacuumed that amount of square feet and then either stopped dead in its tracks until I picked it up and took it back to its charging base, or finished and found it's way back maybe one quarter of the time . I previously had a Neato DV21 that finally stopped working due to some kind of programming problem. The motor was still working and all the parts. It totally spoiled me. If he got stuck, he chirped until I found him, but that was rare. I kept him clean as per the instructions and he did his job every day. I have three dogs, two cats and two teenagers. That little guy really did great. Unfortunately I didn't go with another Neato because there was a great price on the Roomba and I believed the Amazon banner that day that said it was recommended it no longer has the banner by the way . I ended up calling iRobot customer service twice and could get no help to find out why their vacuum wouldn't work as described. The last time I spoke with a customer service representative, she finally told me it was only supposed to clean 325 square feet and go back to its base to charge. When I asked why that piece of information wasn't in the description on Amazon, she told me to take it up with Amazon and hung up on me. So I called Amazon and I described the situation. The customer service representative checked the description, agreed with he, and they, as per their usual great customer service, gave me a label and had me send it back. I'm waiting for my 500 refund and I will be buying a Neato again. You cannot beat this machine or the people who answer their customers. I learned the hard way, don't do what I did and buy a substandard vacuum because of a better price and a banner.

Have had this bench about two days now and spent several comfortable hours on it playing. Can't speak to the durability of the faux-leather, but it looks great. The wood is well-lacquered and shiny. I am a bit concerned that it may chip, but that is speculation and my rating does not reflect this. It assembles easily just attach the four legs with the provided hardware and wrench--no additional tools needed. The storage area is more than adequate to hold several music books it's about 2 1 2 inches deep and runs length and width of the bench. The lid seat does not remain open when lifted, nor does it lock when down, so moving the bench can be awkward. When you grab the seat the natural place to move it into or out from under the piano, the lid lifts rather than the bench. I am training myself to reach completely under the bench to grab wood to move it--that feels odd at this point. Also, the legs have rubber feet on them which precludes easy sliding. Our piano is on a hard wood floor, so I will probably replace the rubber feet that came attached to the legs with slider pads from the hardware store to make it easier to slide the bench. I was torn between 3 or 4 stars and decided on 3 because overall, it feels a bit cheap--looks great, but the 300 limit is probably without a safety factor. I weight about 190 fully dressed and do not feel at all like the bench is near collapse, but there is something about it... The size is also sufficient perhaps for an adult and a child, but definitely not two adults.

Received this item today and just used it. The toothbrush charges in a holder with NO metal contacts. Kinda cool. The overall size of the entire unit is about 2 3 what i expected. On the small side. It does feel pretty cheap, but its a water flosser, not a vcr. If i had to set a price based on overall appearance, id say its a 30 machine. The insides are nearly empty from what ive seen on user videos. Thats ok. The waterpik wand does feel kind of cheap and brittle, and I think if I were to drop it in the sink, I would have a problem. The sonic toothbrush is weighty and has an easy to read LED charge display. But the thing about that is when you put a brush head on the end, it doesnt seat all the way on the neck of the brush, and leaves a 1 16 gap for some reason. I have not forced the brush head on, and don't feel I should have to. It works well though. 2 speeds, lo and hi, as activated by the one button on the handle. Hi speed is pretty serious and emits a clearly audible buzz. The low speed is quieter and more sane at this point. It does come with a couple of toothbrush heads 1 standard, 1 small and a few different water pik heads. There is a holder for all the accessories next to the detachable water reservoir. It is also detachable with its own lid, and has been designed to stand up on its own, which is nice. The water flosser reservoir lasts about 1 30 on speed 2 10. Thats all I care to start out at to get used to things. As time goes on, im sure I will move up in pressure. When the pressure is increased, the motor buzz also increases substantially. If you have a sleeping spouse, this may be an issue. But she should appreciate your clean mouth. Look forward to increasing my technique and familiarity with the machine.

This album should've finally showed the world the twisted, mesmerizing talent of Danny Elfman and co., but rather it quietly found its fans among boingo loyalists and picking up a few among others Tori Amos fans don't ask me why . Boingo is a wild ride from start to finish. Insanity is an 8 minute opus that grabs from all the facets of Elfman, from the wild worldly percussion of boingo past to the symphonic, theatrical side of Elfman we've come to love. Of course, contained withing are the maddening lyrics of Elfman. The song becomes aptly titled as Elfman rages on like a corrupted, insane minister preaching about the insanity of society. Elfman shows a soft side rarely seen before with the songs Useless and Mary, both appearing as beatlesque tributes. Mary is striking both musically and lyrically, as Elfman tells the story of a girl banishing herself from all she once loved because of a burden that lay on her heart. Skillfully written, the listener finds where the character's trouble truly lies through clues placed within the song. Boingo gets loud on Hey! and Pedestrian Wolves, which could stomp all over anything on alternative radio today. The lyrics bite hard as Oingo Boingo add a series of loud guitars to their already diverse musical lineup. Hey is, well, like the opening line says, a commentary without much to say with elfman ranting and raving like the overgrown kid we all know he is. Pedestrian Wolves may drag on too long for some clocking in at around 9 minutes , but it grows more pleasurable after a few listens with its raging guitars with a hypnotic bridge stuck in there. Elfman also creates a very visual picture with the speaker of the song clearly being corrupted by sin by a "couple of pedestrian wolves," taking pleasure on a "soft red cloud of desire." Elfman, here, much like in many of oingo boingo's works, become's submerged in immorality and depravity. The boys have fun, paying tribute to much of the inspiration on the album with a traditional tribute to the beatles with I Am the Walrus, ended with boingo boys being kooky with Tender Lumplings. The show stopper, of course, is the final 16 minute track Change, which is a fun little end to Boingo's career which spanned all of 17 years. The song goes everywhere from loud percussion, guitars, sweet sounding pop, to loud screaming - a true celebration of Oingo Boingo's career.

Received mine in an intact plastic box. The box came with a cardboard sleeve that was crisp and intact. The caliper was in a plastic bag inside the box. When I opened the box, it was lightly coated with an anti corrosion material. I guess this can be mistaken for dried glue. The material wipes off readily with a shop rag. The initial action of the caliper felt somewhat gritty, and zero offset was about 0.003" off. I wiped clean all moving parts, then lightly oiled it. The action became very much smoother after that. Even better, there was no longer any zero offset. So it looks like the grit caught in the caliper was around 0.003" in diameter. Overall, I am quite pleased with the product. The action is smooth without any slop. Just remember to give it a cleaning before using it and you won't have any problems. Minor downsides, the box is large and the foam padding is thin. It is possible for the calipers to rattle around if you shake it. If that bothers you, add another layer of foam in the box. Also, the metal strap holding the depth probe was installed a little off 90 deg. However, that does not affect functionality or accuracy.

The list of great guitarists from the "hair metal" era is long, but there are a handful of names that rise to the top. George Lynch is among the greatest of those who achieved enormous popularity in the heyday of hard rock on MTV. He is best known as the former lead guitarist of Dokken, and later Lynch Mob. What many do not realize is that Lynch is still very active today with Lynch Mob and several side projects. Legacy is a solo record, a showcase of the axeman's skill. Backed by bassist Rev Jones and drummer Michael Frowen, Lynch blazes through four tracks of unrelenting sound, pulling no punches as he announces to the hard rock world that he still has the ability to shred with the best of them. Instrumental albums can at times be a double-edged sword while the focus and spotlight is on the fretwork, there is no lyrical component with which to sing along. As such, the listener can be more easily distracted and miss some of the intricate guitar passages that Lynch weaves into his compositions. There can be no denying the talent that resides in his fingertips, and fans of instrumental hard rock should appreciate and enjoy Legacy from George Lynch.

nbsp Out of the package you will find the Microphone nicely rolled up in a very HIGH QUALITY, leather type fabric drawstring pouch. It is very thick and does a great job at keeping everything safe when not in use! Also Included are 2 Wind muffs, and a very nice Removable Metal clip. The clip can rotate and has teeth that open up very wide which helps it to be able to be clipped to just about anything! The clip hangs Perfectly onto clothing or whatever you decide to clip it onto. I like to clip mine onto my tripod handle, that way it is not visible in my footage! The Microphone itself is 1 1 8 inch long and the cord is 58 inches or 4.8 ft long. It is the PERFECT length for recording with your computers webcam! It's JUST long enough to reach if you want to stand in front of a camera on a tripod and talk. The length of the cord does give you a SMALL bit of room to tuck under your shirt if you would like, but it will have no slack left if you choose to do that with the tripod. I clip mine on mt Tripod's handle and it picks up my voice GREAT! The wire is thin BUT it does feel very heavy and durable. This Microphone does a great job making my voice louder and MUCH clearer than my phone currently does and I use it all the time! The Problem I have is that it picks up It picks up A LOT of ambient background noise! EVERY Single other noise in the room is picked up VERY loudly in your footage! It's especially bad if your trying to shoot video outside, it will pick up the birds chirping or any other noise just about as loud as your voice! The description does say that this is an omnidirectional microphone, which is designed to pick up sounds from every direction and is best for group recordings, where a unidirectional mic will pick up less background noise and is better for recording just your voice. If you have a silent room it works GREAT, but know that if there is any other noise in the room it WILL pick that up as well! I STILL decide to use this since it does such a great job of making my Videos louder and clearer! I did receive this Microphone at a discount for the purpose of testing in my videos and this is my experience and honest opinion of the product and how it worked for me! ENJOY!

Somehow it crunches high notes w o effecting much the low ones and creates a pleasantly mild OD tones. I have read reviews that Vertex Steel String can do something similar at x4 or higher price tag, although has not played it myself yet. Anyway, give this one a try and you may find that the pedal actually worth to be added to your board.

You need these if you have spring-type tube retainers. A dirty secret of the modern tube industry is that most non-vintage power tubes are noisy microphonic, and I haven't met one tube distributor that even tests for that. These little beauties seem thin and fragile and don't seem like they'd do much, but if you have the bad luck of having gotten some microphonic tubes, these will get rid of any buzzing rattling caused by your retainers very effectively. These are the small ones, BTW. They'll fit the EL34 6L6 retainer. I don't think you could use them on anything smaller, not even a 6V6.

I wanted to like this pedal. After all, it looks amazing. But my main problem with it was the difficulty in getting clean tones. I had to put the volume on this pedal all the way down to get clean tones from my strat. And I was unable to get clean tones from my Les Paul. Even at the lowest volume levels on this pedal, my low-wound humbuckers made it clip when playing chords. Yes, I am aware that I can just roll the volume back on my guitar, but I had to roll it way back to around 2 to get rid of the clipping, and were I to play my guitar through my rig at this volume level, it would alter the effect on every other pedal I own, especially my other distortion pedals. For a pedal that touts itself as a preamp or, at least, a tribute to a preamp , there is no clipping meter at all, so you have to use your ears. Because of this, i found it less than adequate as a preamp or DI box for guitar. This also made it very difficult to use this box to record drums though my microphone. To be used as a preamp, you really need a level meter to detect clipping. The fuzz you can get out of this is absolutely nasty, in an 'unpleasant' way. Sometimes, depending on the style of music, unpleasant fuzz can be awesome. But it just was not appealing to my style of music. And any other distortion from this box lacked the warmth or 80s mid scoop that I look for in distortions. On the plus side, it nails that Beatles "Revolution" lead sound, and you can send your guitar signal out to an amp and a mixing board simultaneously.

I've seen many 3D printers now, from the RepRap to the CubeX to the Duplicator Chinese copies of the Replicator and I have to say, MakerBot handles the entire experience the best. This is a bold claim, let me explain. With open source printers, you're almost always expected to use Replicator G, or a similar program, to prepare your prints. This software, while functional, is awful to use. Most printers require fine care involving intricate steps on a regular basis, and with the MakerBot this is at an absolute minimum. The 3D printer world is mostly for hobbyists right now, seeing how extremely they can tweak their machines to perform at the next level. There's tons of forums you could read through on how to build your own, and modify any existing, printer to do whatever it is! The MakerBot, while born from this revolution, has matured into a simple-to-use machine. The software, MakerWare, is a dream to use. Drag drop with minimal knowledge required great defaults for everything. They pioneered ... , a place where you can go to find cool, FREE things to print! The build quality is top-notch, and it's built in Brooklyn, USA. The print quality is friggin' amazing, and only gets better with semi-frequent software and firmware updates. The support staff have ears, they'll listen to your suggestions. What more is there to say? I love printing with this thing. It's as close to a work-horse of a machine as I've been able to find. Absolutely minimal maintenance and setup. It's still part of a hobbyist culture, so there is some work you'll have to do here and there, though the support staff is awesome and makes it easy for you. The starkest difference I've found is that on the LulzBot to change filament a common procedure , you must go preheat the nozzle, wait and check back, then when it's hot enough, open up the software open source Replicator G , and click 'up up up up up up up' in the program for the filament to come out. Repeat for loading the filament back in. On the MakerBot, you go 'Utilities - Change Filament - Unload Filament' and all the steps happen automagically, with it walking you through the procedure on the screen. It's the little things.

I bought this after reading some good reviews here and elsewhere, and got it new for ... . Seems like a pretty good deal? No. The thing is cheaply made, as everyone else said, but that's just the tip of the iceberg. Most of the effects are just about unusable. The reverb has a horrible amount of noise in the background. I'm no guitar tone snob, but the tone of this thing was god awful. Even once I replaced their crummy headphones with my good ones. Also, the metronome is painfully loud if you use in-ear headphones, and only comes in about 7 tempos don't remember exactly . I can't tell much difference between their two available metronome volume levels. The tuner is not a tuner in the conventional sense, but rather a simple note player that you tune to. The settings are all navigated with a voice that distorts a bit based on what settings you have activated. All in all, I just wanted something decent and I was horribly disappointed. Wish I could return it. Don't buy this, or at least without trying it first.

The Hoover UH70935 Windtunnel 3-Pro Pet Bagless Upright is an all-around decent vacuum. It has good suction, is easy to empty and the filter is designed to be rinsed out. It was easy to put together, the directions are easy to understand and it even comes with an indicator light that tells when the canister needs emptied and the filter needs rinsed. I had been using the BISSELL PowerClean Upright Bagless Vacuum, 16N59 and since these vacuums look so much alike, I thought I'd compare the two. The Bissell has the same type of power brush, has the same size beater brush as well as the same type of hose connect. The Hoover is a bit lighter and has a LOT more suction than the Bissell. I vacuumed a small area with the Bissell and then went back over it with the Hoover and was amazed at how much the Bissell missed! Both are supposed to be able to do hard floors by turning off the brush and putting on the lowest setting, but the Bissell seemed to blow more than it picked up. The Hoover sucked everything up with no problems and left a nice clean floor. The Hoover also does a nicer job on edges than the Bissell. I did find the Hoover a little harder to push and I'm guessing it is because of the better suction. I do like the light better on the Bissell. The Hoover didn't illuminate things nearly as well. The Hoover has a retractable cord - no more having to wind or having a cord falling off the pegs! What I like about the Hoover GREAT suction! Easy to use Works well on hard floors Easy to clean filter - reusable Indicator light to let you know when to clean the vacuum and filter out Two brushes - one motorized Easy to turn on off beater brush Retractable 20 ft.cord Easy to read carpet hard floor settings Does a decent job on edges What I don't like The light doesn't illuminate well The non-motorized brush has 3 rubber "fins" instead of bristles The cord seems under-powered for the vacuum and gets quite warm. Would I recommend this to family and friends? Yes. Over-all this is a nice vacuum. I like it a lot more than the Bissell!!
