Flash Cadillac the Continental Kids were a mainstay of early rock roll. They appeared in the film "American Graffiti" and continued to be one of the most consistent working rock bands--always high energy and high quality, with plenty of classic rock favorites and great new charts along the way. Most of the band was based in Colorado in the early 1990s, when an unlikely coincidence brought them in contact with Christopher Wilkins, who was then Music Director of the Colorado Springs Symphony. Flash the band invested in new arrangements to put the Symphony and Flash together on stage. The result was one of the funnest live shows ever. You can see from the playlist that there are a lot of rock roll favorites here. The orchestral arrangements are edgy enough to boost the energy, and the live audience puts everything over the top. Since the development of this show, it has played in countless cities around the U.S., always to rave reviews and sell-out happy audiences. My one reservation with this particular recording is that it sounds a bit dry and the mixing tends to underemphasize and or imbalance the orchestra. Nonetheless, this was history in the making and a lot of fun.

I have went through several chorus pedals with my bass and T-Rex seems to have nailed the great balance of depth and amount of lows with this pedal. I watched a few youtube videos and saw some great reviews of the pedals on talkbass.com, a must go to site for bass effect reviews and anything bass related for that matter. The pedal keeps the low end much better than the MXR or Electro Harmonix offerings and can sit well with other pedals. I keep the depth at about 2 o'clock and the rate at about 10 o'clock with an active bass as a reference. The volume knob on the side of the pedal is what makes it so versatile with several different basses. Basses with hotter active pickups seem to work welll with the volume knob rolled back to prevent clipping and the jazz bass I own with passives can be used with the volume wide open. I found the pedal easy to use and the depth and rate knobs work well. Build quality seems very good. I see T-Rex is well known for quality builds and it shows with the metal case, smooth knobs and good jacks. There are alot of chorus pedals out there with good bass response, I have owned many and the T-Rex is the one I am holding onto. I sold my Boss and EHX pedals when I got this one and have no remorse.

I would highly advise that you do not buy this pH meter. I understand that it could take a few seconds to settle on a number, but this unit just continually keeps slowly dropping by 1 100th of a point and never seems to detect a final reading. I've tried taking the batteries out to try to reset it, and that doesn't fix the issue. Also, I can surmise that there is apparently an automatic power-off feature within a certain number of seconds, and that's not mentioned in any of the instructions. If I could get a reading within a few seconds, then the power-off wouldn't be an issue. Additionally, randomly a backlight turns on and off to illuminate the reading. I can't make any sense out of that, either, especially since it is inconsisten.

I've been a fan of Type O ever since I first heard "Blood Fire" on my Mortal Kombat soundtrack cassette when I was 10. When Peter Steele died, I felt like I had just lost an uncle. But enough with the biography, let's get on to the review... I picked this album up when it first came out, and I honestly wasn't very impressed. I liked the fact that they had gone back to the long, complex song structures, but other than that, I really wasn't feeling it. Recently, I started throwing this album in more often and found myself loving it more and more. At this point, I would have to say that it's my favorite next to Slow Deep and Hard, and Bloody Kisses. Musically, this album is a complete success. Their maturity really shows, and the gothic Beatles-esque melodies that were absent from Life is Killing Me have returned. I would go so far to label these songs as "beautiful". With the exception of Halloween in Heaven it's waaaay too cheesey , I would give these songs 9 and 10 10 each. My absolute favorites are September Sun, These Three Things, She Burned Me Down, and Hail and Farewell to Britain. I may even have to say this album is tied for 2nd place with Bloody Kisses only because this album flows better where BK was kind of all over the place stylistically. All in all, this is a great swansong to Peter Steele's amazingly unique career. Buy this. Set aside an hour or so and listen to this album as a whole. Loudly, I might add. It will not disappoint.

I always carried my violin in a hard-shell case because no way I wanted to damage it. It was a nice tight fit, but didn't have the room that this one does. Obviously this doesnt have the protective qualities mine did, but if you want a lightweight case to either store or take to lessons, this one provides adequate protection at a very good price point. The case is covered with a Cordura nylon, similar to the material one would find on luggage. Theres a double-zippered outside compartment to carry sheet music or a slim book. For example, if you're taking Suzuki, your music and books should fit nicely. Background I own four or five Voodoo Labs Pedal Power 2 Pluses, and probably 70-100 pedals at any given point in time. I have played in bands for the last 20 years, though I always kept it separate from my career goals. I guess that would put me in the "advanced amateur" category of guitarists. I ordered this out of curiosity, and because I could use something cheap for my living room setup where I doodle on the guitar while watching TV . It is much smaller than it appears, and is clearly one of those "made in china" models that gets rebranded a bunch of times under different names but is the same essential unit. Lots of examples of this in the pedal world. I think it's great. It may not be "fully isolated" like my PP2 s, but it's certainly quiet enough for most applications. I put some pretty finnicky stuff on it to see how they reacted and had no problems. In truth, I haven't noticed any noise during all my testing so far a couple hours worth . Combined with its overall feature set and flexibility, and it's a no brainer at this cost. My general bias against the durability of these MIC products means I would never gig with it, that's what the Voodoo Labs are for, but this is a great home practice tool. Recommended.

QUIET! This thing is QUIET! None of the usual "popcorn" from nearly every other stomp box compressor I've known. GENERALLY Dispite the addition of an input pot, I feel this compressor won't get as "Hard" as others, including the earlier MXR model, the Super Comp. I'm still playing with the settings. But I can't seem to get a reasonable Nashville Chicken Pickin Bark out of it. On the OTHER end of the compression, it's smooth as I've ever heard any rack mount studio comp sound. And that's the settings I'm in most of the time. Low compression, just barely bringing down the peaks. Nice smooth jazzy sound on a pretty stock tele. THE BAR GRAPH It's kind of interesting to know when there's compression going on. But generally, the horizontal LED thing is just sitting over there doing it's thing. It's not like you'd want to watch it. Mildly distracting to audience members if they can see your pedals. THE ATTACK CONTROL I've yet to figure out which direction does what with this pot. In the older Super Comp, I think ? it worked in the opposite direction as this newer model. But in both, I never felt like the control does much either in very mild compression or harder Nash Cluck. THE INPUT CONTROL This seems like a fabulous idea. Pre-gain into a compressor that's adjustable. Seems ideal. BUT, it doesn't really seem to have much of a GAIN effect. More like a pot to REDUCE the input. I'm sure it's an op amp stage or something so there should be something like 15-25dB gain. But on mine at least, I use it pretty much close to full clockwise. Otherwise there is essentially ZERO compression showing on the LED's at ANY ratio setting. THE OUTPUT CONTROL I consider this one of the most vital controls on any compressor. On this MXR, like my previous MXR, the "sweep" of the pot seems HUGE. The appropriate output increase is like a 16th of an inch of rotation. NOBODY would ever need full CW or CCW. MXR, PLEASEmake the pot a smaller value so you can truly ADJUST the output. As is, it probably changes 12-15 dB with the smallest possible turn of the knob. No way you could ever do it on stage. And no way you could ever "re-find" the settings by marking the case. The thickness of your mark would be a monsterous increase or decrease in output gain. THE SUSTAIN CONTROL I've never heard it pump, no matter where I've got the sustain pot set. This control might be the only one with a reasonable amount of sweep over it's functional range. It's not insane in one direction and then ten bazillion in the other direction. It does as you'd expect. Shortens or lengthens the reverb tail. THE 4 POSITION RATIO CONTROL I'm not sure what the reasoning is for putting detents on the ratio pot. I'd have to imagine most guitarists want an infinitely variable control. Even if these pre-determined settings are what we all use, I think guys want to twist it till it sounds the way they want, not just click on a number. I'd rather see the more traditional "2 to Infinity" control. MXR seems to suggest that you pick a ratio setting with this knob, and then adjust the input gain to get the compression you want. That seems a little backwards to me. Starting with the gain and tone you want and THEN bringing down the peaks, seems like appropriate compressor use. It's how every studio engineer I've ever known does it. MXR is asking us to first set the compression and THEN adjust the input gain and therefore tone to achieve the squash we want. MAJOR CON Geez! I wish pedal makers would stop putting those insanely bright blue LEDs on their boxes. It's so stupid bright that it literally lights up the stage in a jazz club. It's just ridiculous. Masking tape topped with sharpie tones it down. OVERALL I'm guessing this is MXR's foray into "Factory built boutique pedals". I think what I've got here is a very good compressor for the light jazz peak leveling that I do. But It would definitely NOT be useable for chickin pickin. That's probably the two most frequent modes used in any compressor. So for two bills you get a really quiet box that does 50 of what's required. I'd be very happy to see MXR improve this pedal. Get some more input gain, less output gain sweep, leave or remove the LED bar graph, but surely get rid of the insane blue LED pilot lamp. I'll keep it. Only because it's so darn QUIET. But overall, I'm not real happy about paying 200 bucks for a compressor that won't do nash cluck. Thanks - Lumpy

Who follows my blog knows by now, for this band of Charlotte I have great admiration and a feeling of true friendship as the constant exchange of mail and the wonderful gift I received during their debut in 2009 can confirm, a gesture that I appreciated very much and inaugurated a series of totally unexpected similar events for me. Here is a brand new publication official release Oct. 26 of the new, beautiful album of Preacher Stone, a year and a half after their debut of which I spoke in this post, a year and a half full of success, numerous concerts and awards but that has seen many changes in the original band formed now by the original leaders Marty Hill on guitar and Ronnie Riddle to voice, to which are added the excellent Josh Sanders on bass and Brent Enman on drums. The album title refers to the small but delightful Uncle Buck's All American Pub in Salisbury NC and is a heartfelt tribute and gratitude to the owners of this music pub, Scott Uncle Buck and Judy Howard, great friends and supporters from the beginning and where the band is pretty much at home performing regularly and considering it their den and favorite place for sumptuous eating and drinking with friends. Moving on to listen to this new work we find immediately the sound and style characteristic of the band, confirming that the change of lineup has not affected negatively, I would say that overall you will feel a greater variety of styles and a great overall blend, proposing an excellent mix of Classic, Country and Southern Rock music without getting too caught up in defined labels. To open the CD we find Can not Keep a Good Man Down with a great attack of guitars and a rock blues rhythm that, in my opinion, is the real trademark and style of the band, an excellent and convincing start to the album. Following are Carved in Stone, an emotional ballad that is a beautiful and heartfelt tribute to all veterans, past, present and future, to which is dedicated the whole CD. Come On In immediately brings us back to country rock atmospheres with a sound very close to the roots of Southern Rock, and Early Morning Rise is another track in the Preacher Stone style with scratchy guitars and a really enthralling bass drums base. The aggressive Nuff Said is exactly what I mean when I talk about classic rock music without a defined label just as the next and sweet ballad Hand on the Bible is simply a beautiful song with a wonderful message of love from parents to their children. Funny and ironic, I'll Be Damned is a very nice interlude in pure country style, a prelude to the next powerful and rhythmic Save My Soul, a track that in its second half turns into a sweeping Southern Rock calvalcade with a superb solo by Marty Hill, really excellent and inspired throughout the whole album. The album continues with Don't Take Me With You When You're Gone, another beautiful ballad where country and blues come together in a sublime way, with Ronnie Riddle on acoustic guitar and Marty Hill on dobro guitar as absolute protagonists bring us back on charming and evocative southern atmosphere, typical of the Swampland. At the end of this album there are two surprise tracks, Judge Me Not written and sung by bassist Josh Sanders on acoustic guitar here, a very special and amazing track as it somewhat differs from the style of the entire album but quite pleasant, a further demonstration of the variety of musical moods on the CD, and finally the excellent cover of the famous Beatles' Come Together, obviously in a scratchy, rough Southern version. In definitive a very interesting and enjoyable album, rich of musical styles and atmospheres that will satisfy anyone who loves good rock 'n roll while maintaining Southern Rock as attitude and main reference, a four complete musicians that work together perfectly, an album of wich they're very proud of and that has committed them a lot, but the result is really up to expectations and I am sure will be very satisfying as they certainly deserve. As said the official release is scheduled for Oct. 26, but on their website you can book the autographed album, and on request with specific inscription, a further demonstration of the great availability of the band to its supporters. In conclusion I want to thank very warmly the Preacher Stone for the great friendship and cooperation, especially Zane and Marty Hill, two very special people. Welcome back my friends!

Rob Brown was born in Hampton, VA in 1962. He has been playing the saxophone since the age of 11. He moved to NY in 1984, and since then has been actively leading groups or working as a sideman with Matthew Shipp, Wiliiam Parker, Craig Taborn, Gerald Cleaver, Daniel Levin, Joe Morris, Whit Dickey. Others that Rob has performed and or recorded with are Cecil Taylor, Anthony Braxton, Denis Charles, Bill Dixon, Butch Morris, Reggie Workman, Henry Grimes, Roy Campbell Jr., Hamid Drake and Fred Hopkins, as well as various dance groups, poets, and performance artists. He has toured Europe extensively. He is a 2001 CalArts Alpert Ucross Residency Prize winner and has received many Meet The Composer Fund grants. In 2006 Rob was awarded a Chamber Music America New Works grant. Daniel Levin was born in 1974 in Burlington , Vermont . He began playing the cello at age six. He has performed and or recorded with Billy Bang, Borah Bergman, Tim Berne, Anthony Braxton, Rob Brown, Gerald Cleaver, Whit Dickey, Mark Dresser, Ingebrigt Haker-Flaten, Joe Morris, Joe McPhee, William Parker, Warren Smith, Ken Vandermark, and others. Daniel has recorded as a sideman for Clean Feed, EMANEM, Not Two, and RogueART, and as a leader, for Riti Records, HatHut, and Clean Feed.

I bought an X-50 via craigslist as an inexpensive alternate keyboard to play when I feel lazy and not wanting to lift transport my Yamaha Motif ES-6, a decisively heavier and superior keyboard workstation. At about 9 lbs., it's nearly 30 lbs. lighter than my Motif with its 2 additional program cards, built-in sequencer and sampler. But, I also wanted the great sounds of a Korg Triton, so voila! The X-50. I really love the programs and combis in this synth. Some are HUGE and incredibly FULL sounding, particularly the combis of course. The key bed is on the cheap side, but incredibly you have a lot of range in dynamics when playing the keys slowly fast, much better than any other keyboard I've experienced with this quality of key bed. And, as a former owner of a Sequential Circuits Prophet 5, believe me when I say that the X-50's key action ain't half-bad in comparison. In fact, if you like to "rip," the X-50's key action facilitates very fast runs. In terms of my favorite programs, there are a lot of great pianos, electric pianos, and clavs in the keyboard category, some very impressive strings programs that sound very realistic esp. if you are familiar with how to voice them on a keyboard , acoustic and electric distorted guitars on which you can rip rock solos, and some great sound effects and arpeggiated combis. The 4 very user-friend knobs are very convenient and the std programming provides you the most common adjustments you'll need for a live performance you can also program other effects per your own preferences. These continue to sell at 699, but here at amazon.com the 599 price tag makes this an even sweater deal I bought mine used for 400. All in all, a wonderful little synth. I would've given it the highest rating IF the key bed provided a little better action and feel. But, again, you still get great dynamic range using these keys, so this isn't really a big deal unless you're very picky about the feel of your keys. Again, they are a huge improvement over some of the analog synths I used in the 1980's. If you want the next model up, that would likely be the Korg M50 which I tried out. The key bed provides superior action to the X-50, but just be a little some may consider it enough improvement, however, to seal the deal. The M50's sound generator is from their M3, which is pretty killer, and the M50 has many more features, including a great touch screen, 16-track sequencer, etc. but, its starting price is anywhere between 900 and 1,100. Compare and shop and be sure you get what you want AND need.

I have always used this product and been happy with it for several years . It has been my go-to for everything from spilt milk to a moody cat who registered her dissatisfaction on the carpet. But my latest use has me inspired to actually write a review. Last night my beloved Golden Retriever, Roscoe, was doing what all young, energetic dogs are won't to do, he wanted to play, and play, and play, and play. I easily indulged his whim, because it just meant throwing the ball for him to chase down and return. At some point in his play, he snagged or wore through one of his toenails and began to bleed - a lot. I couldn't leave him outside to tend to it because it had gotten too dark to see properly and he would be beside himself if he wast beside me. So, when I let him in the house, he tracked blood all over my new less than 2 months old silver-grey rug. I ignored that in order to assess and care for the injury. Once done, it was necessary to keep him calm and relatively still to ensure the bleeding stopped toenails will bleed for what seems like a century . Of course, he again took to the area with the rug. I chose not to tackle that issue last night in order to ensure he remained calm and unflustered. We went to bed, he dreaming of frozen doggie treats and I fully aware that my custom rug was now ruined. This morning, more than 12 hours after the original marks were left on my rug, I decided to pull out the Resolve and see if it could at least mitigate the visibility of what was now dried and set blood in my pale rug. Less than 5 minutes later, with minimal effort, ALL the spots were no longer visible. Then, it was just wait for the crystals to dry a bit and vacuum as I would have done anyway. I couldn't be more pleased.

Most of the time a ruler works fine. But sometimes it's useful to be able to get more precise measurements. For those occasions a digital caliper is very helpful. This particular one is a 6 inch model. It's about 9.5 inches in length, but only measures up to 6 inches. It will automatically turn itself on when you slide the calipers apart from the "closed" position. You can easily switch between inches and millimeters inches are displayed to 1 1000 and mm to 1 100 at the touch of a button. You can also re-zero the calipers with the touch of another button. Just be careful not to accidentally re-zero it in mid-use. That is the one design flaw. It would have been helpful if the zero button was recessed to make it more difficult to accidentally press. But that's a minor gripe. The measurements are accurate and precise. It comes in a convenient plastic case with a spare LR44 battery and some technical specs and instructions.

1968 was a year filled with social unrest, and the music in this hour-long CD reflects that. From the blaring guitars and driving beats of the opening selection, "Born to Be Wild" by Steppenwolf, to the angry soulful "Cloud Nine" by the Temptations, on through the mournful "Abraham, Martin, and John" by Dion, this CD will place you in the midst of Vietnam protests, and dismay over the deaths of Bobby Kennedy and Martin Luther King Jr. But even in the uneasy social climate of 1968, respite was found in music. There is the sunny feathery "Beautiful Morning" by The Rascals, the bubble gum hit "Valerie" by the Monkees, and the gorgeous slow-dance soul ballad "Lalah Means I Love You" by The Delfonics. This CD focuses on the hits, so if you're looking to delve deeper into the music of 1968, look elsewhere. But it is an essential CD for anyone who wishes to build a thorough music collection from the 1960's.

Item was received quickly, well packaged, and as described. Dimension provided in description are accurate. If you are looking for a low-cost solution for displaying items then this is your case. If you are looking for a case that will last years and years this is not your case. The lightweight paper construction makes the item inexpensive, but it lacks durability. So if you're using this to display items at shows or markets the wear and tear will be evident in relative short order. The batting on the inside fills the space but is of a thin density so you may want to front it with fabric covered cardboard to make the mounting of your display more rigid if not items will shift. The clips that secure the sides do their job and secure it snugly.

A handy device. As others for these type units have indicated you'll be disappointed if you expect it to read within a couple of degrees of the actual temperature. The utility comes to the foreground when you compare DIFFERENCES. I use mine to check for places where the insulation or weatherstripping is not doing its job. Like during the summer when you can check the ceiling and walls for warmer spots - indicating insulation needs some looking into to cut down on your A C costs. Same idea for the winter when looking for cold areas around doors windows, ceilings too. Anyway, the point is use it for DIFFERENCES. If you want to read absolute values be prepared to shell out whatever it takes to reach your accuracy requirements!

This is a great screw pitch gauge that will last years and years. It's as good as Starrett but less expensive and soooooooo much better than the knock-offs from China that you find in the big-box stores. I found one day I needed to measure a metric thread in the 0.075mm to 0.025mm range and although my Chinese thread pitch gauge had the blades marked with the very fine pitches the measuring edges were so poorly done that I couldn't find a match. I looked at the edges with a strong loupe and found a profile that looked like a mouse eaten piece of cheese. When the Stahwille gauge arrived I looked at the finest threads with the loupe and the profile looked like an illustration for Machinery's Handbook. Had no trouble determining what the pitch of my mystery screw was with this gauge.

I am a believer in the fact that music must be able to stand on its own. If music doesn't stand apart from the picture then it usually means it's a weak score. Flip this around and it goes the same for the picture. If the image cannot stand on its own then it's a weak image. Only when the two standalone mediums of storytelling combine is when something magical happens. For A Warrior's Odyssey we get something truly special from composer Penka Kouneva. Penka has worked on composers' teams on many blockbusters such as Transformers, The Matrix Revolutions and Pirates Of The Caribbean At World's End. This album is a standalone album, but it's inspiration and emotion is deeply rooted in images. Over her many years composing for films, TV and games she has amassed many different genres under her style. What this album does is express all that in a uniformed action experience. She channels mostly her work in video games and her work with Steve Jablonsky to craft a true musical expression. The music here is telling a story that could have been plucked out of a film or a game. The arcs and the emotions are all flowing and they carry the listener on a terrific journey. Her European voice comes through loud and clear as she is definitely channeling her human experiences growing up in Bulgaria. She blends those classical sensibilities with modern sounds and textures that demonstrates a very well honed control over the music. The melodies are simple and elegant, but have a strong emotional presence. It's very easy to add layers of textures to music and have it become a jumbled mess, but that's never the case here. The action tracks are structured terrifically with rising and falling action that keeps the tempo moving when it needs to. Then we have a few quieter tracks that build subtle emotions such as "Forgotten Steeples", which happens to be one of my favorite tracks on the album. The fact that this is not a score to a film or a game she is able to incorporate some more varying styles and sounds, but it still manages to blend into one beautiful journey. A Warrior's Odyssey is a must listen for anyone who loves the blending of classic sensibilities with the modern sound found in films and games today. Kouneva establishes a strong central voice for what clearly has her deep personal stamp on every note of music. The album is structured very well and within 30 seconds you'll be sucked into what feels like a narrative score to a film. A wonderful effort and a wonderful experience for the listener.

I like the texture of this cereal, and the taste is pretty good, but considering how much sugar it has, to me personally, it doesn't taste very sweet--I augment the sweetness with stevia. However, it does taste good with fresh fruit and milk I use almond milk , and I like the fact that it has lower levels of calories than other, sweeter granolas. A big plus of this brand of granola is that it is certified organic and is non-GMO. It is a high-fiber cereal, and it has at least some protein per serving when most cereals have almost none. Please note, though, that this granola is not gluten-free. As granola cereals go, this one is a bit lower in calories than most. A serving is listed as 3 4 cup, rather than a serving of 1 4 cup or 1 2 cup either of which tiny serving sizes is a sure sign of very high calories in a particular granola . The total calories per serving are 260, but you can minus out 20 calories of that because a serving contains 5 grams of fiber. There are 10 grams of sugar, 10 grams of fat with zero trans fat , and 6 grams of protein. All in all, I like this granola enough that I would purchase it again. Ingredients Organic rolled oats, organic evaporated cane juice, organic soy oil, organic brown rice flour, organic pumpkin seeds, organic flax seeds, organic oat syrup solids, tocopherols, sea salt, organic molasses, organic rice bran extract, and organic cinnamon. Allergen Warnings Contains soy. Produced in a facility that uses wheat, peanuts or tree nuts.

Purchased this elsewhere in lefty, and was impressed enough to want to review it. Having quite a few guitars and strat types, I took a chance on this just based on , and very impressed what you get for what you pay. I have 2 other glen burton I'm pleased with on same principle, one jumbo acoustic. One semi hollow electric. These original standard strat clones are going away replaced by the hurricane I believe, which I know nothing. But I know strat clones and this is a good one. Here's why... Bucker in bridge. Sealed die cast 10mm tuners. Solid ash body import ash decent maple bolt on with an impressive fretboard finish at this . Excellent fit and finish overall. These are the weak areas, Plastic nut So-so pickup quality, but not as bad as others in genre.. Standard import tremolo with cheap block...useable but not pro by any means. Basically a fantastic starter guitar, and with 3 relatively simple upgrades, as good as 5x spent. And they can be done over time, as you can budget or as you learn. The overal weight, balance, and playability is as good as most much higher priced. Ship factory fresh and shiny with normal accessories and paper on strings which I'd suggest changing a.s.a.p... And action may be a little high an easy bridge adjustment will get it decent. In my case all I required, a fender mim bridge with steel block 18 bone nut 6 And changed string trees 8 . I'm ok with rest for now...overall plays just fine, get some wilkinson singles later and maybe do some wiring later...be on par with most and not have to baby it because it's big .

I have had these sprayers for about eight months now, and so far, they are the best sprayers I can find other than what is available through the medical industry. I have even used them for acetone with far better than expected results. After about two months, the bottle itself became soft and malformed, however, the sprayer still worked like it was supposed to. The one way check valves kept the sprayer primed, so from the first pull of the trigger, it was spraying. Also, the spraying action was smooth and consistent. There has been no gaps, or trigger sticking in either direction up to this point. The O-ring does its job and has prevented any leakage from the bottle, even if it is held upside down. One other feature I like about these sprayers is that there is no annoying drip from the nozzle, even when I bring them from a cool shaded indoor environment outside where it is hot and they are put in direct sunlight. It would be nice if they had a larger spray capacity, the only area where the "SPRAY MASTER" has them beat, other than bottle size, and that is only if you can get the "SPRAY MASTER" to spray a full trigger pump . Also, like anything that is not either durable metal or glass, certain chemicals, such as acetone will eventually break the sprayer down. Finally, I would recommend ordering just the sprayer, and get the "SPRAY MASTER" bottles to put your chemicals in. The sprayers mentioned above are more durable than the bottles they come with. The "SPRAY MASTER" bottles however, seem to be the most durable on the market, and hold twice as much product.

My teenage daughter and I have been trying to find an easy, ready to go protein shake that we like. I'm trying to drop a few pounds and she has a very hectic summer schedule and barely has time to eat most days. She doesn't care for most protein bars and doesn't want to resort to junk food since she's trying to make healthier choices as well. This banana cream shake from Svelte gets a thumbs up from both of us. It tastes great. I'll try to describe the taste but I'm not sure what to compare it to because I've not had many banana flavored drinks. It has a real banana flavor, not artificial or syrupy, with what tastes like a milk base. It's not too thick like some shakes. It's about the consistency of whole milk. It's not too sweet either. We both love the flavor. The fact that these are organic, have a long shelf life, and do not have to be refrigerated although we prefer them cold are all added bonuses. These are a little more expensive than some other protein shakes but they really do taste great and are worth it. They did a great job of curbing my appetite and helping my daughter have the energy to get through her busy day. We are definitely ordering more and planning to try these in some of the other flavors as well.

Do NOT buy this microphone boom. Unfortunately, this is the first disappointing purchase I have ever made on Amazon. I was searching for a relatively inexpensive desktop microphone mount and thought that this might do the trick. All seemed well The arm itself was sturdily constructed and the clamp did a solid job at locking itself onto the edge of my desk. The problem, however, came with the apparatus to which you attach you microphone.r I have a Blue Yeti microphone, and this could not hold it. Before you dismiss my comment above, allow me to tell you first and foremost this was not a weight issue. What I found was a problem with the item itself. In the image of this mic boom provided by Amazon, you will see a thin rod attached to that microphone holder. As you might expect, it moves up and down. This is done with a screw in between the rod and the arm.. The screw was much too loose, and try as I might, I was unable to budge it. At first, I thought it might be because I was simply too weak to turn the screw with my screw driver. I let my roommate, who is significantly stronger be bench-presses around 300 pounds have a go at it. After attempting to tighten the screw for around 5 minutes or so, we found little to no success. With that being said, this will not hold your microphone, or any other microphone for that matter. The faulty construction of this device fails to serve its ultimate purpose. There are several other options on amazon that are a bit more expensive, but I strongly recommend you spend that extra buck to save yourself from utter disappointment upon receiving this unusable contraption, you will not regret it. I understand if you are not convinced by my review. Perhaps only MY microphone boom was defective, and the one you will receive will not have the same issue--perhaps YOU will be strong enough--stronger than my 200-to-300-pound bench pressing roommate--to tighten the accursed screw. Those are all valid arguments, as I cannot say that I had bought another one to examine if this is a recurring problem. However, I write this review based on my personal experience, and it is my sincere desire that what happened to me does not happen to you at all costs. I cannot stop you from purchasing this item, only you have the power to stop yourself. In this situation, you can either be your best ally--or your worst enemy. However, I will not leave you empty handed. As I speak I have discovered alternatives to the desktop microphone arm. As you may notice, the mic arm stands within the two-digit price range are somewhat lackluster for both their own individual and overarching reasons. The only arm I found to have unanimous pleasure derived from it came from the RODE PSA1, which sells for 99. I have come up with another alternative to the desktop microphone mount A purchase of a microphone floor stand with extra purchase of a telescoping mini-boom attachment. Of course, that's only my suggestion. If you do find another desktop microphone mount that is to your satisfaction, then I congratulate you on your successful purchase. I, on the other hand, did not want to take any more chances with yet another faulty product. I am returning this hunk of metallic trash.

I have both the SE Custom and the Paul Allender and appreciate both guitars for what they can do. Both guitars because they are the SE series are made in Korea and have obvious problems. The biggest being finger fret boards, nuts, and lack of detail on the finishes. The Paul Allender is a very powerful guitar with its EMG 81TW and 89R Dual mode Pot split active pickups that will give you pretty much any sound configuration you would want. I have about 60 guitars that I have acquired over the last 20 years and have just recently purchased these two as Paul Smith finally decided to enter the mid range guitar market where all the money is and stop relying on the sale of 3K to 8K guitars. I believe Paul has a way to go to catch up with people like Epiphone in producing flawless offshore guitars as this does not happen over night and requires specific manufacturing processes be put into place and then strict line inspections be done on a constant schedule. The first Paul Allender I got, I sent back because of a crack in the Ebony Fretboard which was very dried out. I am waiting on the replacement as I write this review and will follow up on what I get It's the purple one . The Custom had to have conditioner applied to the Rosewood fretboard a few times to bring it back to life and bring the frets even with the board edge which stopped the fret edges from ripping up my fingers. The nut had to be ground down on the ends as it was to tall and caused problems with first fret barring. The nut was off center and the fretboard was sticking out on one side at the top so I ground this down. My point in all of this is that at one time you would expect small problems like I have mentioned from mid range offshore built guitars, but that has changed in the last 5 to 10 years and you can expect near perfection from a guitar costing 600.00 to 800.00 made in Korea or China, or Indonesia or wherever. Actually, I have an Epiphone Nighthawk Custom reissue that puts either one of these PRS SE's to shame as far as fit and finish are concerned at under 400.00 and made in Indonesia.

While my house was being painted one of the front exterior light fixtures was accidentally broken off at the stem where the glass enclosure attached to the base. The fixture was made out of cast aluminum. I managed to attach the outside part to the base using a two-base epoxy but the stem for the light socket that holds the bulb wouldn't hold with the epoxy because I couldn't get them to match exactly and it was difficult to position the piece. I looked around for a different adhesive and discovered this reinforced epoxy putty, the idea sounded enticing. Bought a tube of the product and followed the instructions which basically goes like this, cut off a piece of putty to the size you think you will need then begin to knead the putty to a uniform color the putty consists of an dark activator core and the outer lighter colored putty layer . I molded the putty around the stem and to the base, you have about 4-5 minutes to get the putty into position before it hardens to a hard metal-like substance. Worked like a dream in my case. Recommended for those hard to achieve bonds. EDIT I deleted the word 'steel' from my review based on the comment I received.

----Pros---- A black hole of sound. You speak into it, and absolutely no sound comes back. Seems to absorb far more sound that simple acoustic foam, which is why I purchased this product. I have these mounted in 1x4 pine boxes, wrapped in acoustic fabric. Works very well, kills off a lot of sound in my room. They are very dense, and are fairly heavy. Not too heavy to lift, just a tad awkward due to their size. Although, I have my completed boxes hanging from two picture nails in drywall without an issue. ----Cons---- A bit expensive. If I did this again, I'd go with the 2 or three inch. I got the 4 inch hoping to avoid bass traps. The cost to benefit ratio is questionable. Not sure if that counts as a con or not. I have a feeling that some very low frequency sound does make it through, although it's still better than foam or nothing at all. I haven't measured it yet, so I shouldn't say for sure. I say get one of these packs for your bass-heavy areas and stick to the 2 inch everywhere else.

Far away from the light and sound of the city, a lone man on an island of steel and glass stands and looks through a great telescope. He wonders, wonders about the great many things that reside in the night sky, of the stars and space in between. He wonders about the great many things about which he used to wonder, about the falling stars and the sometimes-stars and the stars that were always moving. He wonders, watching as a small little speck of light shifts slightly, flickering out and reappearing in the blink of an eye. He had wondered once, as a child, why some of the great bright stars twinkled in the night sky. And now, he wonders again. Deep below the island of hard metal that floats heavily on the waves, a fallen star stirs in its slumber. Within the star, a people rises. And from without the star, a new life bursts forth. If you're planning to use this product for home-recording that can be done, such as conference calls, this mic is very solid choice to go for. This mic will provide sound quality as good as any 50 70 dollar headset microphones from my experience, and having to buy 3-4 of them for 3-4 different people in the room will not be cheaper option. The sound quality of this product goes down further due to it not having direction and being too sensitive. Obviously, these problem occurs due to microphone being too sensitive, so using post-production programs on your recording will definitely help this Compress and EQ will be necessary If you have a sound-proof studio this microphone will provide you excellent cost efficient work, however, at that point you might want to go for 100 microphones such as SHURE SM57, or Blue Yeti depending on your use. As a professional live broadcaster, I have gone through more than 20 microphones and headsets, and this was my first non-headset microphone. This microphone worked for me when I had desktop on ground, somewhat suspended from vibration using carpet. However, I was not able to use this microphone with laptop due to fan noise that was 1 2 2 3 as loud as my voice even when I had it at furthest point from laptop on table, or even when I had desktop on my desk. Make sure you have very quite room and make sure you do not have this microphone near any source of noise, including your PC. Having your PC on same desk as this microphone will cause enough noise to microphone that your listeners will not enjoy.

This ukulele is more sturdily built and durable than other ukes I tried in the same price range. Because of this, it is a tiny bit heavier than some. This may or may not make any difference to you. The feel of this instrument is the same as a typical acoustic guitar. Thick, glossy, smooth finish over the entire instrument. Most ukes have a natural finish on body and neck. This one has a bit less traditional finish. Ukulele purists might not care for this instrument. Good materials spruce top, and the rest of the uke is made of rosewood. One issue I've found with several other ukes than I don't have a problem with on this one is sharpness of fret wire edges. I really don't like playing any instrument with sharp fret wire edges. No sharp fret wires this one. The sound is the loudest I've heard coming from any concert uke at this price 99 . It might not be the tone you are after. Some folks might like a mellower tone than what this uke produces. I sometimes feel like a mellower tone, and might buy a uke made by a different company someday. The other ukuleles I've tried in this price range were made by Cordoba 2 Cordobas.. same model with two different finishes as far as I could tell.. sharp fret edges on one but not the other. Mellow, quieter tone. The one without sharp edges was a good instrument , Kala felt kinda flimsy, tone ok, some of the Kala's had the same fret wire issue , Luna seemed to be made to look cool hanging on a wall. Not good sound or build quality and Peavey. The Peavey was somewhat well, if strangely built. Small sound hole on front off to the side , larger sound hole in back of instrument. Weird. A few fret edges on that one as well. Pretty good sound from the Peavey, except it might be too quiet for some people. The only downside for me regarding this instrument is that when my tinnitus ringing in the ears..a medical condition is particularly severe, the sound of this instrument is a little too sharp. If I get more serious about playing the uke, I'll likely buy a different one that is quieter. One last thing worth mentioning.. this is listed as a 12 fret uke. I don't know why. The instrument has 16 frets. Overall, I'd say this ukulele is awesome, especially for the price. Check out Mitchell acoustic guitars as well. I had a 12 string guitar made by Mitchell that was rather good. Since, after a lot of practice, I'm still not rather good at guitar, I sold the 12 string, but not because I didn't like it. Just could not play it or any guitar well. Recently, just for fun, tried an acoustic-electric 12 string Mitchell at a music store.. lovely tone. Ok then, thanks for reading,

This little device can pack far more than a little punch. You have this devious toy in your hands now what do you actually do with it one might ask....? It's quite versatile really. Move it gently across the landscape of the body to experience a variety of sensations all over, from head to toe. Add a bit of pressure while rolling over the same areas and you will find a good number of variables. Not to mention sensations. Now give it a third go round.... adding a great deal of pressure. This experience changes the entire playing field. It's important to note, this tiny wheel can stand up to most any thing. On the other hand the human body can not. While taking a closer look you might thing that with the slightest bit of pressure this thing could easily perforate the skin w ease, as if through butter. While this isn't quite true. It's important to be aware of just how delicate the skin is under you new devilish dainty. With a little practice you can use this with great effects. Although the learning curve can be a whole different story. I highly recommend experimenting on ones self for a bit before using this toy with a partner. Unless of course this particular partner has a great deal of experience with this type of toy to begin with. AND you excel at taking directions and ready nonverbal cues. This toy is easy to maintain..cleans easily w a little soap and water. it's wise to dry it well after cleaning to keep it in tiptop shape. This isn't for the faint of heart. Although with a bit of practice you can see a HUGE variety of applications. Enjoy. Remember, safe shine, consensual wins the race.

First I'll start off by saying the only pop albums I own are by Michael Jackson and Paramore. Im not a very big fan of this kind of music in general but if I listen to an entire album and enjoy basically every song on it the artist must be doing something very right with it. Now if you make the assumption based off of the album name that most of the songs are about being in love you'd be right. It's not really varied in subject matter but it's the way the songs are put together that give away the shift in mood from song to song more or less with an 80's 90's feel to them. My only real complaint about this album is Warm Blood, I don't think it's bad song it's just when the slowed down distorted voice comes in during the first part of the song that soils the rest of the song for me. That one thing makes Warm Blood the song I like least off of this album unless I'm thinking of the japanese deluxe release, where the remixed version of I Really Like You is the worst thing on the whole album without question as basically all included remixes always are. I have yet to hear one remix worth the time to listen to but overall E-MO-TION is a great pop album and a good album in general, slightly more so if you don't have the remix of track 3 on the version you buy. The three songs that stick out the most to me are tracks 6,7, 8 with track 8 being my favorite of the 3 partly because of the traces of Bonnie Tyler in Carly's voice when she hits the chorus. Still if reviews leave you on the fence about whether or not to purchase the whole album do what I do, go to YouTube and look up full albums or find a playlist for the whole album give it a listen. From someone who barely ever bothers paying attention to other types of music besides various forms of Rock and Metal I would recommend this to anyone looking for something good in pop music. I do believe reviewing music is impossible since a review is meant to be objective and music is about feelings and not being lifeless in trying to describe it. All we can do is give our biased opinions on it and hope others will feel the same whether it's for or against.

I also appreciate the wider area around the fingerboard and scroll. Because many triangular cases are narrow there, it can be hard to fit the shoulder rest into the case as well. Fortunately, this case has enough room to store the shoulder rest. Rosin goes into the covered compartment at the very end. There is also a small blanket to protect to put over the violin when you place it in the case. Negatives The shoulder strap for the case is not particularly functional. It is oddly placed and makes it a bit awkward to open the case sometimes. I don't think it would cost them very much to fix it either - it's just a slight design issue. There is also only one strap rather than two, so my daughter is unable to wear it like a backpack, which she did with the case for her previous violin. The bottom line here is that the strap is awkward to use, so you never use it, so then it just gets in the way, but there isn't an easy way to take it off either. One more thing is that the hard plastic cover on one of the feet fell off within the first week of using it, and I couldn't find it again to glue back on.

The Big Blue Filters have been around for years. This one is "big" and it is "blue", but "Big Blue" is a trademark of Pentek, and this is NOT one of their products. I didn't read the reviews, so I missed the fact that this is a knockoff - I returned mine, and here is why. My purchase was a replacement housing, so it needed to fit the original head the black part on top this knockoff did not match my threads. And the O-ring on my original genuine Big Blue is "captive" - it rests in a groove at the top of blue housing. On this one the O-ring "floats" - it can move around, side-to-side - which could make it harder to align for a good seal. And as far as being a "deal", you can purchase the original Pentek 150233 Big Blue on Amazon for another 5. That's what I did - and it fit my threads sealed perfectly.

I purchased this for myself to take some piano lessons. I ordered it late evening on a Friday, and was surprised the following Saturday afternoon that the item had already been shipped and would be arriving Monday instead of the estimated day of Tuesday. It arrived that Monday afternoon, the sender took great care in packaging the pieces together and protecting them. I opened it up and set everything up with little effort and was off and running. As for the keyboard itself, it sounds great and is built about as well as you could expect for a relatively inexpensive keyboard. I haven't had any problems at all with the construction itself, but you can tell it's not a top of the line model. It does have touch-sensitive keys which is a plus if you want the experience of playing an actual piano on a keyboard. Hooking it up to my computer was fairly easy. The instructions ask you to wait until it installs, then if it doesn't work right, install a specific driver downloaded from Yamaha. I'd advise to skip the Windows driver setup, as it doesn't work at all, and just go ahead and download the driver and install it to begin with, it'll save some headache. On that note, I prefer not to use Windows at all, so the fact that you can only use the keyboard and subsequently, my lesson software with Windows on a PC anyway, MacOS works as well I believe is a bit of a turn off, but not a deal breaker. I'd also prefer to be able to transfer my user songs to my computer a little more easily. All you can transfer from the keyboard itself is the backup file which contains everything including all the songs and there's no way to really extract that from the computer to just get the song I want, so I'll have to use another type of software to record the output of the keyboard and get my recorded songs that way. The stand is just great, it adjusts easily and keeps the keyboard safely upright. I'd imagine if you kept the keyboard in a middle of a room, it'd be relatively easy to knock over, but keeping it out of the way against a wall, I don't foresee any problems with that. The headphones are good for listening to the keyboard through them, bad for everything else. As far as the software that comes with it, all I used was the USB cable which works as expected, I do not have a review for the software as I didn't use it at all. The power supply is a cheap little plugin, it works for its intended purpose but is not a Yamaha branded power supply, and the light on it indicating that it's powered up is way too bright if you ask me, it's so bright it created shadows on the ceiling of the room it's in, causing a little panic when my wife woke up after having a nightmare and saw the shadows on the ceiling. Anyway, it's a great purchase for beginner piano keyboard players, I'd recommend it.

I've been an avid electric guitar player for the past 6 or 7 years. When I went to college it become a hassle to have to keep transporting my guitar, amp, and effects back and forth between home and school during breaks. I had always been throwing around the idea of buying a cheap acoustic guitar so I could keep my electric guitar and all associated equipment at home while I was at school, but never found something I was willing to buy. But then, lo and behold, came Amazon's Cyber Monday deals and I picked up this cheap little note full-size classical guitar for 60 and free two-day shipping with Amazon Prime. My guitar arrived the next day a nice little surprise . I tuned it and started playing, and I'm surprised with how good a 60 classical can really sound. The tuning pegs are a little....off....but I can forgive that. You get what you pay for, certainly, but with this thing you get so much more than you expect. Summary Buy it if you're looking for a cheap guitar to mess around with or even if you're just starting guitar. This thing is absolutely unbeatable in this price range.

Musicianship is top notch, overdubs are minimal and there's even some audience participation. If you like Frank's music, you need to get this. To add more of this sort of entertainment to your library, pick up "You Can't Do That On Stage Anymore- Volume 2 The Helsinki Concert ". It's the perfect companion to "Roxy Elsewhere"...the same band playing a lot of the same material, but even faster...

Great DVD, GREAT SOUND, GREAT BAND, weird surroundings small audience, DVD 5.1 audio by Steven Wilson! This is quite a massive dose of instrumental madness. The sound is spectacular. The picture is great but don't expect concert-style lighting as there is none. It is similar to the visual feel of Greg Koch's "Guitar Gristle DVD". In some ways it's good, because it's bright and you can see the performances well. Camera work is very good. Govan is a little Vai, a little Holdsworth, a little Beck, a little of everything,-even country! He could be accused of over-playing at times but this is just a ridiculous demonstration of chops by all three musicians. I was initially dissappointed by the "interviews" from the bonus section, but others may find it to be a unique approach. They are more along the lines of instructional informational segments pertaining to the songs construction. Basically a glimpse into how they work which is a new twist I would rather have seen some conventional interviews as well, I would like to know more about Govan, the band, and their influences etc. Given the greatness of this DVD it is nit-picking, but it is a little hard to hear Govan when he talks during his interview segmentas. That segment could have been recorded better. This is a MUST purchase for any fan of GOVAN or any fan of exotic electric guitar playing. Fusion fans, shredders, and instrumental guitar freaks will love this DVD! BTW In addition to the DVD you get two CD's as well! Update After watching this for a second time the idea of what they were trying to do on the interviews has grown on me. I was clearly wr.. wro.. wrroong. This is actually an interesting and unique approach. I Still would liked some conventional interview segments, but I see what they were trying to do and they were right actuslly! The bassist was particularly interesting. It was sooo wild seeing that he records on an old BOSS hard disc recorder. His honesty gives hope to those of us who employ many of the same techniques in recording as he does albeit in my case in a much more simplified form Guthrie missed the mark on his "Interview" segment as the other two guys were more interesting. This DVD is just packed with great playing. So many tunes it is brain-damaging This DVD is going to catch on. You really need to invest in this as it is the gift that keeps on giving.

This product is an effective, high wattage SCR. My end-goal was making an enclosure that allowed me to control the burns while making Lichtenberg figures in wood. I bought this product as a low-cost alternative to a variac because the waveform was not important for my particular application, but buyers should be aware that SCRs chop the output voltage's sine wave. It is not recommended for loads with high reactances, as mentioned in the description. In order to monitor the voltage and current, I combined this unit with a DROK digital multimeter, which also was easy to use and very accurate. My only real complaint about this unit is the lack of a convenient means of grounding the case. It is achievable, but a case-ground lug would be an awesome addition. Besides this, I'm very pleased with this unit and will be buying from DROK again.

"Berket" is the second CD from the band Edessa. Edessa named after a town in northern Greece is a California based band that plays adaptations of traditional music from Greece, Macedonia, Bulgaria and other parts of the South East European region sometimes called the Balkans. The band offers an authentic but fresh representation of the folk music from that part of Europe. The band features Lise Liepman-accordion, Ari Langer-violin, Paul Brown-bass, Rumen Sali Shopov-percussion, and a number of other musicians who sit in on select songs. The real focus is on George Chittenden-clarinet, saxophone, zurna, gaida and guitar. Chittenden excels when playing the clarinet and saxophone, with beautiful soaring notes and unorthodox rhythms. Edessa seems to like exploring the elements of Balkan music most at odds with the Western music most of us are used to, but it does so in a way that should make the music accessible to those with an open mind. Those familiar with the folk traditions from that part of the world will be impressed with the music authenticity and the excellent musicianship on display throughout the CD. The music is as beautiful as it is otherworldly. The two longest tracks, Djevrias Dance Saskin, 12 40, and Parakalamos Halasha Mou, 13 16, may test the patience of even the most ardent fans of the genre, but that's a minor complaint. Overall this is a fine recording and well worth purchasing if you are interested in the music from that part of the world.

This is an historic recording which was in the catalogs for decades. It first appeared as a two volume 78RPM set on Columbia records as the first of a series of operas recorded in complete form by The Metropolitan Opera and as far as I know the first opera to be recorded in English in the United States. When Columbia came out with their then new LP format it was immediately transferred and eventually, finally, saw light on their budget Odyssey label. The are certainly many fine editions in the current crop of "Hansel and Gretel" recordings as well as many fine DVD's of the work. This is the recording I grew up with and the fact that it hasn't seen light as a CD release is amazing to me if only for it's historical value. It's also a damn fine performance!

A well recorded documentation of one of the shows on the Reunion Tour of 2005. Hughie Thomasson and and Henry Paul sound like they did in 1975. Those wonderful Outlaws harmonies are still there thanks to Chris Anderson singing the parts of the late Billy Jones. Anderson also plays Billy's guitar parts rounding out that famous "Florida Guitar Army". The sound is rounded out by David Dix and Monte Yoho on drums, Randy Threet on bass and David Robbins on keyboards. The set list for this show included all the important songs such as There Goes Another Love Song, Hurry Sundown, Green Grass and High Tides, Gunsmoke, Stick Around For Rock N' Roll, Ghost Riders in the Sky, etc. Added to the mix are two from Henry Paul's catalog, Grey Ghost and So Long. There is also a beautiful version of Prisoner in homage to Billy Jones. If you missed this particular performance or any other on the Reunion tour, this CD will let you experience the next best thing..

For McGrath's second CD since his 2010 debut Long Shot, the tenor saxophonist presents a riveting musical portrait of the past four years of his life, reflecting upon "family, friends, and the human condition" through six appealing originals plus a standard and a hymn, all intended to evoke feelings such as "healing, love, loss, dishonor, spirituality, and gratitude." Born and raised in Puerto Rico, the now Chicago-based McGrath's career as a musician and educator has taken him to many far flung places, including New Orleans, Mexico, Speculator, NY, cruise ships in the Caribbean and Mediterranean, and back again to Puerto Rico. McGrath studied at Northwestern University, as did his quartet mates on this album-- pianist Joaquin Garcia, bassist Kit Lyles, and drummer Gustavo Cortinas-- and their polished techniques and resourceful creativity can be seen a testament to the quality of the Jazz Studies program at that school under the direction of Victor Goines. McGrath's prelude to the opening "Night and Day" delves into tone rows that barely hint at the melody of this project's sole standard. His delivery of the theme is subtle and tender above Lyles' bass vamp, but with a more forceful bridge. Garcia's engaging piano solo ranges from deliberate to gushing, while McGrath's is transfixing, insistent post bop. A thrilling arranged section for unison tenor and piano erupts finally into a clever excerpt of "Night Has a Thousand Eyes." Cortinas then displays his energized rhythms over Garcia's vamp and montuno. For "Maybe, Please Don't Lie?," Garcia's long, reflective intro recalls "Blue In Green" in its harmonies and spacing. However, McGrath unfolds a theme that stands on its own in terms of lyrical spirituality. Lyles and the leader each expand upon it in the understated passion of their respective improvisations. Daniel Iverson's popular hymn, "Spirit of the Living God," is launched by Garcia's gospel-tinged piano before McGrath soulfully delineates the theme and provides a robustly expressive, sermon-like solo. Garcia's own turn exudes a feel-good soul jazz attitude, after which Cortinas offers his thematic and spirited slant in trades with sax and piano. The title tune "Martha" and this recording as a whole is dedicated to McGrath's influential grandmother, Martha Albelo, and the gentle theme is softly and lovingly intoned by the saxophonist, with his backing trio's sensitive support. Lyles' statement impresses with its deeply resonant sound and heartfelt phrasing, and is followed by a succinct Garcia and more expansive McGrath, the latter partly unaccompanied and emotionally driven. "Patty Cakes" contains a shimmering free form opening, graced by Cortinas and Garcia's pleasing textures, and succeeded by McGrath's playing of the rhythmically provocative ballad melody. Garcia's solo elaborates upon tone rows earlier introduced by him, flowering into a full blooming rhapsody. McGrath's subsequent ecstatic, churning effort is relentlessly probing and enormously moving. "Sileray" begins dramatically prior to the sweet-toned theme, within which an urgent staccato motif provides contrast. McGrath's exploration exhibits a multitude of mood changes, whereas Garcia's maintains a lyrical focus. "Rue Nov. 7" is almost like a continuation of the previous "Sileray," but with a more uniformly insistent outpouring of emotion, as conveyed by both McGrath and Garcia in their audacious go-for-it improvs. A free form contrapuntal tenor-piano conversation, encouraged by Cortinas' stabbing drum patterns, is an effective surprise coming just before the reprise. On "Kintsugi," McGrath unveils a multi-faceted theme full of motifs that inspire enthusiastic and affecting solos from Garcia and the composer, the tenor's more extended and one of his best on the CD. A brilliant section then unleashes Cortinas alongside a manic McGrath-Garcia vamp.

I'm 58 years old and have been playing guitar for over 43 years . I made the switch to Acoustic a few years ago and was using a Fender FA-100 which wasn't a bad guitar for the price but, I wanted to upgrade to something much better. I've always wanted a Taylor but always thought they were highly expensive and out of my price range so when I heard about the Big Baby and it's price, I thought I would give it a shot. I'm really glad that I invested 400 bucks in this one. It's easily the best sounding and well made ,Acoustic I have ever owned . The body is a tad smaller than your average sized, dreadnought, but at my age, I find this to be a relief on my back. Also the neck on this one, is nice and thin and makes playing a relief compared to other Acoustics with their thicker necks. The sound and tone on this is much comparable to instruments costing 3-4 times more than the suggested retail price and when I got it in from UPS, it was already set up to my personal specs and strung with a new set of Elixirs , which was a huge bonus . Since then, I've discovered Taylor to be the best company to deal with as far as customer service is concerned. If you haven't done so yet , go to their website and register your guitar which will make you eligible for their contests and their Quarterly Magazine " Wood and Steel ." I love my Big Baby and am now saving up to buy one of the Academy series. IMO, once you go Taylor, you'll never go back to playing anything else.

I have played synthetic reeds exclusively for the last two decades. Every year I'll buy a box of cane reeds, and every year I scream about the fact that 1 in 5 will be playable, even after doing my "reed rituals." I decided to try the Legere Signature on the advice of a fellow sax player whom I esteem. I play a 3.5 Fibercell reed on my vintage 80s Yani tenor mouthpiece, facing is 9. Pretty open, which typically should be played with softer reeds. I tried a 3.5 Legere and it was unplayable. So, sent it back and dropped it down to 2.25. SUCCESS! Just tried it on two different pieces...my Yani 9 and my Ernst Schreiber Heavenly Jazz 8 . Francois Ligature on both...the reed is exceptionally smooth across all dynamics, and altissimo was playable, but took a little adjustment on my part. After a little time spent with upper register and transitioning to altissimo, it was a piece of cake. Synthetic reeds aren't for every sax player, but for me, they're perfect for Jazz, Big Band, and Rock. I don't play a lot of legit music on my tenor, but the Legere Signature reed 2.25 I have on my alto is terrific with concert music and classical, using my ESM and SR Tech pieces. I highly recommend this reed for any player looking for an alternative to the rampant imperfections of modern cane reeds. NOTE I'd suggest playing a few sizes .25 increments down from your current reed, since these run a little harder in my experience.

I bought two of these devices and have noted a peculiarity. When placed side by side and given time to stabilize, they either read the same temperature, or they differ by two degrees. So far, I have not observed any temperature reading of an even number. That is, when the two change from displaying the same temperature, one will quickly shift to a temperature either two degrees higher or two degrees lower, never displaying a temperature one degree different from the prior reading. My guess is that the actual reading is measured in Celsius and the converted to Fahrenheit. If the precision in Celsius is in one degree increments, the precision in Fahrenheit would be no better that 1.8 degrees, rounded off to two degrees, since the unit does not display tenths of degrees. The humidity displays of the two, when in the same location, have never been different by more than one percent. For the price, the units are satisfactory.

Was looking for a good solution to practice guitar at night while my 1 year slept. Before I was plugging in headphones to my amp head, but it was getting kind of annoying to set everything up take out electric plug covers, get all cords out of the cabinets, etc . Oh how life changes with a baby! This little product is the perfect solution. It takes two AAA batteries some generic brand are included in the package . There are two ON settings regular ON and "FX" ON. Regular on is basically like a standard lead channel. Turn down the gain and the cleans are pretty clean. Crank up the gain and you get a nice rock crunch. Nothing heavy metal, but a nice crisp crunch. FX ON turns on a little delay effect. You can not adjust the delay effect, only turn it on or off. There are also knobs for volume and tone. Plug your headphones into the side, and you can also plug in an MP3 player on the top to jam with a backing track. VOX has samples for all of their AMPlugs on their website, however they seem to be missing samples for the Joe Satriani version. I would say that the JS version is pretty close to the "LEAD" amplug. I have a pair of middle lower range headphones Audio-Technica ATH-M30 Professional Studio Monitor Closed-back Dynamic Stereo Headphones and the amplug sounds fantastic. In the title I wrote "wait for this to go on sale" ... b c of the JS branding, this particular amplug retails at a much higher price than the other amplugs. However, it frequently drops down to the same price level so keep your eyes peeled! Personally at the higher MSRP i don't think its worth it, but at the same price it is if you are looking for a nice lead tone.

I ordered the PICO ATM MINI fusetap to permanently wire in a Garmin GPS in my 2011 F-150. My truck uses the LP Low profile Mini fuses BUT this standard MINI fusetap WORKED PERFECTLY in my 2011 F-150. The Low-Profile MINI fusetap does not work in my truck fuse panel. The fuse panel slots are extra deep around the fuses and won't allow the LP version fuse tap to seat. Glad I ordered this PICO standard MINI fusetap. It has longer "legs" on the fuse and worked extremely well. I went to O'Reilly to get the extra MINI fuses 5A and wired my GPS in. I used the fuse position for the heated seats since I don't have leather or heated seats. Also that location allows the fuse tap to sit correctly in the fuse panel and only had to slot the fuse panel cover side a bit for the side exit of the pigtail cable. Works great and I now have a ignition switched power connection for my Garmin. No more unplugging the cable every time I use it!

Koreans strive to be the best they can in everything they do, and often feel something produced in Korea might not be up to the standard of other countries. It has meant Korean music has made little impact on the world past pop weirdos The The. If you mention the name Crash to even the most devoted of metal fans, few will know what you are talking about, which is a shame. Crash are the out and out leaders of Korean metal. Forming back in the late 1980s, it took until 1993 for this album to hit the shelves. And what an album it is! Imaginative, cutting death thrash in the Kreator the album title is a bit of a clue and early 1990s Sepultura mould. Anyone managing to stumble across this will discover thrashing riffs by the truckload, a rock solid rhythm section, and even blast beats. The song writing is excellent. Crash's single, and still their signature tune, "My Worst Enemy" is an excellent demonstration of what the band is all about- big riffs, unrelenting power, anger, aggression, and excellent musicianship. Yes, I know I've just used the word "excellent" three times in two sentences, but this band justifies such praise. There are two versions of "My Worst Enemy", sung in English and Korean, with the Korean version coming across the more convincing of the two. In places, the sound is perfect Sepultura, and could easily have been lifted off "Beneath The Remains". An Heung Chan's throaty roar matches Max, except with a slightly different accent, and he seems to cope with English better. An's bellowed version of "Smoke On The Water" is more than passable. Guitarist Yoon Doo Byong also throws an original solo into the old metal standard. By 1993, thrash was on the outer, which may explain why this never managed a wide release outside Korea. Perhaps if it had, the Korean metal scene would have earned the same respect as scenes like those in Brazil and Poland. When you look back at some of the utter crap signed up by record companies in search of the next Metallica or Sepultura in the late 1980s and early 1990s, it seems incredible a band such as this could have been missed. While a little behind the times, Crash deserve more attention internationally than they have received.

OK... first off... this is a vulcanizing repair kit... not a hard epoxy or fiberglass. It is sandable when it has cured fro at least 24 hours any earlier and it just shreds like a plastic shopping bag but it does not blend or feather well. When applying this product for a tub repair, you want to be fairly spartan with it because, like I mentioned, it is a vulcanizing epoxy, so sanding it down is not a good idea. The main reason I gave this product a bad review is very misleading in what you get. The description reads thus "1.06 OZ, Fiberglass, Porcelain Plastic Repair Kit, Bonds Fiberglass, Porcelain, Plastic, Ceramic Tensile Strength 1500 Psi, Water Resistant, Handling Set Time 30 Minutes, White Or Almond. This product is manufactured in China. Excellent for industrial and domestic use." But you don't get a fiberglass repair kit... you get an off-white 2 part vulcanizer NOT glass resin with a small piece fiberglass cloth in the kit. Including a piece of glass matt does not make this a fiberglass repair kit. I guess I need to learn to read better... as soon as I saw "made in China" on the kit I knew it was going to be garbage, and I was not surprised by the results. I own a small handyman business and if I would have used this on a customer's tub, I'd be repairing it again at my own cost. Don't buy this product unless you want to incur the same BS results. Just go buy a small fiberglass patch kit at the hardware store. You'll be a lot happier with the results when you're done.

I had purchased this item to get sound from my XBOX ONE to a pair of headphones. Due to the fact the XBOX ONE does not support Dolby as of 02 22 2014 I went on a search for a proper converter that will convert the audio signal to stereo. After buying SANOXY AGPtek Digital Optical Coax to Analog RCA Audio Converter Adapter it DID NOT WORK, that device does not convert the optical audio to stereo. For the most part if you see a box that looks like the SANOXY box I will tell you that they do not work on the XBOX ONE. What the Orei DA21 does is a real conversion and allows stereo audio with headphones with RCA or a 3.5 jack EX earbuds,on ear or over ear headphones . I'm using a pair of Afterglow Universal Wireless Headphones. All I did was plug in the optical cable toslink to the converter and then the power supply to wall outlet,once that was done I plugged in my RCA cables to the converter and just like that it was done..... .... ... .... yeah thats pretty much it. T.v volume can be lowered or raised and does not affect the audio for the headphones. I believe Remo make the best djembe outside West Africa. Having owned West African djembe I changed over to key-tuned Remo djembe due to the simplicity and reduced cost in changing skins. Rope-tuned djembe are easy enough to tune up but need someone who knows what they're doing to apply the skin. The Remo key-tuned djembe enable ease of use in this respect. I have a Remo Mondo key-tuned 14" djembe and that is a much more professional instrument than the Apex. The musicality is significant whereby the Mondo has a much deeper bass and nicer tones and slaps. The Apex plays well, but just feels 'cheaper'. The Apex does fall somewhat short of the quality sound that genuine African djembe or the Remo mondo djembe produce. Nevertheless, the Apex will get lots of use and I will not be concerned if it gets knocked around a bit as its winning points primarily come from its apparent durability and toughness.

I'm using this to connect my MIDI keyboards to my MacBook Pro running GarageBand 10.1.5. So far so good. My Yamaha S-80 1999 and a Nord Electro 2 2006 communicate well via the MD-BT01 and work flawlessly as controllers. No latency issues for me with these keyboards. Best of all, and the reason I bought this interface, is that my vintage Yamaha SHS10 battery powered keytar 1981 also works. It only has a MIDI out but that is not an issue other than the unit dangles, which might be hard on it over time. The MD-BT01 has turned this cheesy 80's toy into a full on wireless controller. How cool is that! When I first plugged it did have a .5 second latency issue but by clicking reset midi drivers in GarageBand preferences see how to below fixed this. I think maybe this is what other people who have had bad latency issues need to do to solve the problem. A couple of steps to help with set up 1. Down load the phone app MDUD-BT01 form the app store and update the firmware on the MD-BTO1 2. Open Audio MIDI utility on the MAC, then open the MIDI studio and click the Bluetooth icon to select connection 3. In GarageBand under preferences choose audio MIDI tab and reset MIDI drivers.
